speaker: Sam Schrager
words: This conversation with Hattie Wilkin Johnson took place at her home in Kendrick on August 4th, 1976. The interviewer was Sam Schrager.

speaker: Hattie Wilken Johnson
words: Well, that's what comes down to the potlights from up on the camera area.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Is that where you were born?

speaker: Hattie Wilken Johnson
words: I was born there. And then, let's see, there was Frank and Mary and Herman. They were born before I was up there. They were some of them that lived down there, the Pine Creek. And that was Rancho Father. Got the homestead. It was, you know, when they went around and surveyed some of those fairs, they filed on some of them. And he bought that though. It was in 1886 they came here. My father and mother came from Minnesota. They came, they were trained down to her perforia. You know, they had to come on the Great Northern and the Northern Pacific. They came from Spokane and they went down towards Jefferson here just almost right away. But of course they were on that other, that's where they had the lottery ticket. And then from Downton Perry they came up on a boat to Luskin. And from there up, one of the ship first down there, they brought them up here on the hill. They got to Juliero and then came up on the, up here on the hill.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: How did they know where they wanted to go?

speaker: Hattie Wilken Johnson
words: How did they know that's... Well, they talked to the Watcherford brothers up there. There's one homestead on each side of where Father's Place was just etched in there.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: The Watchers, they wrote to him and told him that that was the place that he could finish up to, oh, what do they call it?

speaker: Hattie Wilken Johnson
words: You didn't get that land, you know, living in it. Not homestead. Homestead, yes, they homesteaded it then. And the Watcher brothers, they wanted him, you see there's one on this side and the other one was on this side. And they were in there and then for the others, it went down to the pot ledge and there was another man had it homesteaded over there.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Were they friends or, were they friends from back in Minnesota?

speaker: Hattie Wilken Johnson
words: No, they were back from Germany, I think. The father came from Germany to Minnesota in 1872 and then he and Mother weren't married until 1876.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Was she from Germany too?

speaker: Hattie Wilken Johnson
words: No, no, she was born in Milwaukee. Her mother and her grandfather, they come by boat, I guess, down to New Orleans and they come on up the Mississippi River up to Milwaukee. And she lived there until she was 14 years old. And then her folks moved to Dakota, Minnesota, right close to Dakota across the line there. But they moved to Minnesota.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: To do what?

speaker: Hattie Wilken Johnson
words: My father came. He didn't come until two years later after Mother and then they were there already. And then he came from Germany and some more German folks there that he was acquainted with. And then they worked there and he didn't have any land that he really landed and worked around there for until 1886 when he came here to Idaho. Then he always set it to a place and then of course he had his farm from there on.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Where had your mother's people come from?

speaker: Hattie Wilken Johnson
words: They came, my mother's people, they came from Germany but just where, I don't know. My nephew's been trying to find out. Her mother had been married before and she had a, oh, she had one boy and a girl and her husband, he died with a, with a, a, a suction. And then she lost a little boy too with that. And then she, her father was living there and she said she just couldn't stand to stay there in Germany and she brought that little girl and her father. And they came around by boat to New Orleans and that's where they come up to Mississippi to Milwaukee. And then she married this, this man up there. I have to look at him for a minute. Oh, that's okay. You know what, that's it, but anyway. Just when, it doesn't matter. And he had one daughter too. Uh huh. And they lived there, there, we'll walk, until my mother was fourteen. She was born then and some of the brothers were born there too before that time. So I don't know, they must have came there about eighteen. Or fifty sometimes because my mother was born in 1860. And, uh.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Well what year were you born in? How? What year were you born?

speaker: Hattie Wilken Johnson
words: 1897. In 97. Uh huh. That had, it's been quite a while ago but then another year. I couldn't believe it when I came over here. I never thought how old I was until, uh, three years ago, it'll be three years this fall. I was up on my ranch near the river, you know. I just, just living the life of the ride. And my husband passed away in 1950. But I had lots of friends and we'd go, and my nephew that, he's been here, he's writing a little story about the whole Wilburn family. And he's going way back, getting all the dates and data all brought around. And he'd come up every fall and after his father passed away too, that was my brother. And of course his brother came up too. And then we went on a trip back to Montana. That falls through the Rocky Mountains every fall for about ten days or two weeks. We just took it all in. Well it was, I enjoyed it. I enjoyed my place up there. I just had a big garden. After my husband passed away, we didn't have, I just kept one cow and then I raised the cows, you know, the heifers. But I had chickens and all up to hill. I thought you had to, had pneumonia with the flu, you know, that's flu going around. And then of course the neighbors said that I shouldn't try to do that. But I lived there. Had raised a big garden. Had lots of fun.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Can you tell me about growing up near Cameron, what it was like when you were a youngster?

speaker: Hattie Wilken Johnson
words: Oh, I, well we were all busy and all working. All the children, they had to get in and help. We went to school and we had a mile to go to school up at Cameron. And there's quite a few of the girls here. There's five of us. There were until this last year. They started school the same time at Cameron, at the old schoolhouse there. About the real old one, but then it was fairly old when we started school. And well, we went to school. First it was seven months of the year, then eight months, then finally nine months when we finished up there. And well, we, in the wintertime we'd put on our leggings and high -end galoshes to walk up there. There's the snow sometimes like that. We studied and it was really the teacher, there was Whitliff Smith for many years. And he really made us study.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: What was his first name?

speaker: Hattie Wilken Johnson
words: Whitliff. Whitliff R. Smith.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: He made you study?

speaker: Hattie Wilken Johnson
words: Oh yes. They had the classes. My little morning we had, the first thing was reading and then spelling and arithmetic. We had that before noon and then we had geography. And then I have to get up in the physiology, history, and of course I had singing along there too. We had just everything and we had to take our books home and study it.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: I still got the old geography, would you like to look at it?

speaker: Hattie Wilken Johnson
words: Oh sure. I brought some of the things over, I hadn't unpacked everything after I came over here three years ago. So we stayed here until 1898 when they got the ethics of the railroad to go on down.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: You mean the railroad was stopped because it was Indian land?

speaker: Hattie Wilken Johnson
words: Well they come up to here, that was Indian land and then they went back and forth and they got a lot of old people coming down. They had a carrying green up there or anything. People were going to Spokane or any of the stops from here on up to there. And then it was after that, of course I wasn't old enough to remember that, that my sisters and brothers, you know, they told me about it. They had big hotels down here, three of them. And my older sister was seventeen years older than I was because she worked down here, she was coming from a hotel. And that is after I was older that I remember, you know, when she was down at nineteen or so. Or nineteen hundred I mean, she had a three years old nineteen hundred. And the father and mother, they were married twenty -five years, is it nineteen or one. You know, all that length of time. I just remember all that. And they had these, there were so many of those men, they come here and they were building the tracks. And of course, they started building, they come down the green down there off the ridge and then they got busy and they built this, Walter Goss rode up towards Cameron. And it was, I remember I could just say to them, you know, I was just a little girl then. Of course I remember going down to Kendrick with them in the buckboard or sometimes in the wagon.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: What was the ride like? What was the ride like from Cameron down here?

speaker: Hattie Wilken Johnson
words: Oh, it wasn't very good. It was a narrow road and they had a turnout at the side over the edge of the canyon, you know, that comes through down the lower place. And of course they had the floods up there, you know, flash floods, you know, they just washed that out and that road just didn't the same. But the road is good now that goes up there. But it was steep, you go around for a hill and then another, especially if you're stuck on a grain truck or a grain wagon, not a truck but a grain wagon. Four horses. You had to be having a good driver up there to keep them.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Was it scary for a little kid?

speaker: Hattie Wilken Johnson
words: It was scary. It was scary. I come down there, oh, when I was about 11 or 12 riding up on a grain wagon up high. They floated up high and there were lots of grain wagons to come from down here to the warehouses from all the way up to Southwick and way beyond that. They'd go down in the morning and most of them made two loads a day. Except on moment one where they lived back up for them and they'd be just one day there'd be one and the next day there'd be two loads that they'd come and then they had this shoot that was over in this corner where the pilot makes its bend. And they'd shoot it off the top of the, they'd go into the way up there on the hill and set it down at the warehouse.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Did that come off Potlatch Ridge?

speaker: Hattie Wilken Johnson
words: Yes, it came off the Potlatch Ridge and there were two warehouses down below.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: How far was the top of the shoot from Cameron?

speaker: Hattie Wilken Johnson
words: Well, over the shoot goes from Cameron was about three or four miles. It went on the other side of where we lived and then over, right along the top of the hill.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: And then to the shoot?

speaker: Hattie Wilken Johnson
words: Yes, and then down to the shoot and the shoot was just in this corner. Here when you go out you look where that river makes, Potlatch makes that turn. It was right in there.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: By how big do you think that pipe was?

speaker: Hattie Wilken Johnson
words: There was no pipe. That was ropes or wires or whatever. I don't know what it was. But up there. And they'd come down off that and the wheat tracks would come down off that pulley. Oh, it was on a pulley. They would go down and then they'd go back up empty and they'd put more wheat sacks on. They'd just keep that. I see. So it was just a pulley system. Have you been down from North St. Davenport, you know, they used to have those shoots come down over the hill with the grain. Yeah, I've never seen them. No, never seen them.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Did it work very well?

speaker: Hattie Wilken Johnson
words: Oh, just fine. And then they didn't have to come down this far. You see the grain trucks, the grain leggings, actually. So they weren't trucks then, but the grain leggings. Four horse grain leggings. They'd go up and down that potlatch shoot. That is the grain there through Cameron and distributed around the top of the ridge. I've thought about the dust at the AD. And then we sit right on the corridor, one of them on the crossroads down there. And then one of the sheep's rowing on us from Kendrick and all that dust come up there to the house. They went by both ways you got the dust. Yes, they come down from Cameron to here and then on down to Kendrick and the other one about this way and that way to the sheep's road. If you ever want to go drive up there. I'm going to. I don't know that Cameron. You should go up there. Of course, Cameron isn't what it used to be, but the church is up there on top of the hill. That's where the schoolhouse used to be too. And there were a lot of people. There's a store, most of them. This is an old little town. They had a blacksmith shop that did lots of work up there.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Blacksmith shop and then there was one general store?

speaker: Hattie Wilken Johnson
words: Well, it was the store and the post office together. It wasn't just a general store. The postmaster, he carried about everything that we'd leave there until we'd go to Leland. And that was quite a store. They had, I think, two or three churches there. And there's later on when I grew up and Father used to raise lots of bees up there too. He had up to fifty stands and he sold lots of honey and the honey and the corn and the extracted honey too.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Was Leland a lot bigger than Cameron then?

speaker: Hattie Wilken Johnson
words: Well, it wasn't time. They didn't have a school at Leland or close to Cameron. They had a school for quite a while. But then there was much more, many more people that came up and settled around Leland. And then it got to be quite a town. It was a bigger town. They had one or two stores, general stores. They had a drug store, blacksmith shops and hardware stores. And of course the generals, the dry goods and everything else they had there. You could buy anything there. The daughters and daughters, they were there for a couple of years. They had a flour mill up there. Van Pelt's. They ground the flour up there.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Did your father take his wheat down to Kendrick or did he use the chute?

speaker: Hattie Wilken Johnson
words: Well, they brought it down here, down to Kendrick for the wagon because we were right at the head of the grade. And he told me he had to enter this warehouse down here. He was a great fellow too. That is, as I look back, when he came he was building the roads, the road over here for I think many years, about 20 years. Up there. And then he drove to Lawson down where the commissioners met down there. At that time the committee, the three commissioners, they included Orofino and here and down at Lawson. And then of course it came so that they took the Orofino that was put in a new, that was the Idaho.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: A separate county you mean?

speaker: Hattie Wilken Johnson
words: County, yes. A separate county. And then it was from here down to Lawson, all around. And then the schools, we had, I think in this county there was 56 schools. And the superintendent, she would make the rounds to all of them once a year.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Well, did your father farm very much land?

speaker: Hattie Wilken Johnson
words: Well, 160 acres. And of course we raised a lot of things on that. We had cows and, well all of it was in except for just a little canyon that was resolved in farmland. He raised corn.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: He raised corn for the Lillie Seed Company, have you heard from that?

speaker: Hattie Wilken Johnson
words: They sent the corn for him to raise that and just kept it absolutely by itself. It's the best corn that they had. Then the man from Lillie's, he came to look it over. I was about that big and of course I got to ride along out there to the fields.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: They used the corn for seed?

speaker: Hattie Wilken Johnson
words: They used the corn for seed. They took every bit of the seed, every bit of the corn, what was husk, the lillies. They took that to their, where they had their company at all, they thrashed it out and all and they sold that seed.

speaker: Hattie Wilken Johnson
words: Well that's real interesting because I thought that hardly anybody did grow corn here for a cash crop.

speaker: Hattie Wilken Johnson
words: Well, they had it for a cash crop too, but mostly to grind it.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: So your father's corn was that good?

speaker: Hattie Wilken Johnson
words: That was their best corn. It was a new corn that they had. It was a special grade, you know, that they got. He raised that 20 acres, of course that was a lot of seed.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Did he raise that for many years for them?

speaker: Hattie Wilken Johnson
words: Not many years. I don't remember him. He raised four. But that year I remember that especially because when he drove up, they had livery barns down here. They had two livery barns. And the men, they would drive them out and some of the men, the drummers, you know, they'd go and make their own for their soybeans and stores and all. And they'd usually go up about Monday mornings, of course those kids would be down there at the road to ride. Catch a ride up. But the drummers in the warehouse down here, they were the ones that would bring them up. And that was it. The fellow that came from Lillies, he got one of the weeks that he came up and we rode out. And of course I rode along with Father. I remember Father went to him when I was small. And everybody had a chance, you know. But I remember that just so well. And I remember when we had to get it. Now that was in, I imagine, in 1901 because my brother just older than I was. And Father, he, they hold that, all that corn in my hand, you know, with the hoeing. And the little kids had to go and pull the weeds around for around the corn socks. So I had to really remember that. But they had finally done real well. They paid him well for raising that corn.

speaker: Hattie Wilken Johnson
words: These drummers, would they go around to Southwick and all the... Southwick.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: And you would ride with them?

speaker: Hattie Wilken Johnson
words: Southwick.

speaker: Hattie Wilken Johnson
words: And then you'd go down around Balleelum, get a order of fish, teakens, and all the other little towns. And they'd have a question that was so long. You'd go up the river here, up the foundation, you'd go up there.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: You would ride with them?

speaker: Hattie Wilken Johnson
words: Oh, not just to school. Oh, just to school. Just to school. They'd come up the grade and just to school at Cameron. No, I wouldn't go any further. I didn't always make it down there if I had to do a little bit extra work at home. We just had time to get up there and time for the, for 9 o 'clock to go easy up there. If they'd get up to Fun Life, get away a little earlier, if they'd come up a little earlier. No, they went around and then they'd go down the other way through Balleelum and back down to Kendrick.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: What work were you supposed to do at home before school, before going to school?

speaker: Hattie Wilken Johnson
words: We had to get out and bring the cows in or maybe after we got older, they had to help me up. We had to wash dishes and had to get all help in the house. I don't know, there was always something to bring in after we'd come home from school. Father always chopped the wood, he had to chop, we had to pack it in so the porch, it'll pile it up. It was all chores, especially getting the dishes washed and everything we could do before we'd start school.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Did you have work when you come home in the afternoon too?

speaker: Hattie Wilken Johnson
words: In the afternoon, yes, we had to get right in and get the wood in. I didn't bring home all of our books, this geography was one of them. Not this one, but this is the advanced geography. We had to bring it home because we had so many lessons that we had to get in and learn them. After supper we'd wash dishes and everything and have to get in there and do some more studying. Then, until the weekdays, we'd go to bed and time would come and we'd put ourselves away until we'd get up early in the morning. Then, Saturday evenings, Friday evenings, Father and the neighbors, the women, they'd come to you and the women, they'd carpool and visit. And then they'd play solo sextet, I don't know if you know that. No, no. They'd sit in the kitchen and of course, Mother had the living room. And they didn't, Father never allowed anybody smoking there, Mother didn't like smoking. But out there in the kitchen, I think, they did smoke. The men were smoking out there playing cards. And then on Saturday evenings, though, they had the angel said, or the sundown of it to church, they'd ring. And of course, after that, why we didn't do anything, we went to bed. Father said we had to go to bed so we'd get up early in the morning to go to church.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: They would ring, what would they ring on Saturday evenings?

speaker: Hattie Wilken Johnson
words: That was the bell up at the church on Saturday evenings. It's the angelus. The angelus. We read about that angelus. I think so, but I don't remember what... It's the angelus. Well, it's when they ring, you know, the church bell.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: It brings in the Sabbath, brings in the Sunday?

speaker: Hattie Wilken Johnson
words: That was for the Sabbath coming the next day. It was about sundown. I can remember looking and seeing the sunset. You know, when Father would always stand looking to the east then. And of course, after that, why we didn't do anything, we'd get ready to go to bed after. And of course, in the wintertime, why, we wouldn't go to bed right away, naturally. But then we didn't do any... The kids didn't come there to play cards or anything. But Sunday evening. And that was the evening for my brothers. And the brothers were older. And they had all the other kids that came around, the boys. And they'd sit in the kitchen and they'd play cards too. And of course, I'd get my lessons in the front room and all. Sometimes I'd slip out. Of course, they had all different names for us. I thought they were playing. I'd never do anything about poker or anything like that. But I knew about the other games or what they would tell me, you know. I got pretty catty at playing with that. And I'd win and then finally they'd go out and pull my hair and I'd get out and slip back in the front room. But it was lots of fun. There weren't any of the girls that came down to play. But I played with my brothers, you know, before. So I knew something about them. The boys would come in. Well, there were quite a few. Maybe eight, nine, ten of them. What were the...

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Do you remember any of the card games? What they were called?

speaker: Hattie Wilken Johnson
words: Well, it was poker though, after I found out later on.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: You didn't know it at the time?

speaker: Hattie Wilken Johnson
words: It was poker and then it was, oh, 21. And, you know, I don't know what they called it otherwise. It was 21. And then there was a Chinese that arrested them down at most. You know, they'd catch them playing at the Chinaman. You know, they'd spit out their gown there.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: What was the name of that?

speaker: Hattie Wilken Johnson
words: I don't know what the name was. But you played them. But you played them. Oh, I played it. And then after later years when I was down to working down in Portland, the girls, we worked at the Multnomah Hotel. And we had an hour of being half an hour later. And then after that, we'd sit and play cards. And we played just about all the games. And then that one, it was a Chinese one. It was a China, but he was from the elevator up and down. And he was talking about it, you know. Down at most, you know, he used to, not too long ago, and there were quite a few Chinaman down there, they were arrested if they'd catch them playing that game. It was a lottery game. It was truly gambling.

speaker: Hattie Wilken Johnson
words: So I knew how to play it. And you were pretty good at it when you were a youngster at these games.

speaker: Hattie Wilken Johnson
words: Oh, I thought, yes.

speaker: Hattie Wilken Johnson
words: I just came in handy.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: You beat the older boys?

speaker: Hattie Wilken Johnson
words: Oh, yes, we were the boys. That is, there at home, it was always the boys, there was no girls that come down. But I was always getting...

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Were girls kind of not supposed to play card games?

speaker: Hattie Wilken Johnson
words: Oh, they could play cards, but then they couldn't come out on Sunday evenings so much unless the folks came. Sometimes if there'd be some of the neighbors come, there was a milk piece up there, the wyvers, they'd come. Why, we'd play, but we wouldn't play those games then.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: They kept a tighter rein on the girls than on the boys?

speaker: Hattie Wilken Johnson
words: No, only the girls. They had to stay at home and get...

speaker: Hattie Wilken Johnson
words: They'd study for the next stage, going to school and all, but no, the girls didn't go. You know, we were 10, 12 years old, we didn't go anyplace. Unless the folks we met were the folks. So the boys kind of had more freedom, they could get around and do more. Well, the boys did. They went more, the boys could go, but of course they were older too. My brothers were older. You see, one of them was, he was three years older than I was. And the other one was about five years older, or six years older. And of course they were the ones that did the playing, the other ones, they were about that age.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: You said that your father stood to the east when the sun went down? You said your father stood and faced the east when the sun went down?

speaker: Hattie Wilken Johnson
words: Well, the angels, you know, well of course the church was up there too, the church bell. And when that church bell was ringing up there, why he was... Of course, I guess maybe he was brought up that way, back in Germany or however, they listened to that.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Which denomination was that church?

speaker: Hattie Wilken Johnson
words: We're at Lutheran Church. But they don't do that anymore, they don't ring that bell all of a sudden about Saturday evenings. They did until about 1920, I think, until a different minister came.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: What was the Sunday routine like? Did everybody go to church in the morning?

speaker: Hattie Wilken Johnson
words: They all went to church that belonged to the church, all of the kids. They went to the white -headed team or buggy, or some of the heptakite and mice buggies, the other one not so much. And the rest of us, we walked the same as we did until school. And my father and mother, it was muddy or something like that, or snow on the ground, and then they went up to the team. But otherwise, we walked.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Did you dress in best clothing?

speaker: Hattie Wilken Johnson
words: We had our good clothing on, high overshoes, and had our new coat on, our good coat. Well, I guess it was the only coat we had and we wore it to school too. And then, of course, they were all caps that we wore, usually, or a tie or scarf that went all around our head.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Was it Sunday school or regular service?

speaker: Hattie Wilken Johnson
words: We had Sunday school. Get there in time for nine o 'clock, until ten. That was Sunday school. And then they had the sermon after that, it was up until eleven thirty. So, it was a real church service. They then sat on one side and the women on the other. Of course, later, when the new ministers came in 1920, from then on, of course, then it all mingled. And then when the war broke out after the war, then they had the English church once a week, once a month. They had English church. Before that, it was German. And then it went on and finally, they had the German service just once a month. And then, of course, Good Friday and Monday, Thursday, they had the young folks, the communion. And Friday, they had the elderly folks. They had it in German, usually, until, of course, now there aren't any of those old -time people up there. Maybe one or two.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Do you remember?

speaker: Hattie Wilken Johnson
words: Sunday school, they had this in the back of the, oh, Sunday, of the, the Hemlock, the Hemlock. And it was in there. And then the minister, he explained that all to us, then we'd have to remember and tell him. I, I don't know whether I have any of this. This is a picture taken down here of Kendrick.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Of the family?

speaker: Hattie Wilken Johnson
words: No, just the half of the family. There was another brother came after that. There was another card like that. But at the end, there were the five older ones. These were the five younger ones. So they draw this other brother to him.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Can you see me over there? Which one are you? Which one guessed?

speaker: Hattie Wilken Johnson
words: Just make a guess.

speaker: Hattie Wilken Johnson
words: That I looked like?

speaker: Hattie Wilken Johnson
words: This was a... Well, I never would have known.

speaker: Hattie Wilken Johnson
words: Those were the five that were born here in this area. This got in, actually, in Idaho, and the other one sent her from... Well, no, this older sister, though she was born in Minnesota, she's a Penman. By the lot, I think I've got it that is where we were married.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Was Luther League mostly for young people?

speaker: Hattie Wilken Johnson
words: Yes, that's young people.

speaker: Hattie Wilken Johnson
words: And, and we had it every, every two weeks. And we'd study and we'd have all parties, about a party, we'd have lunch. I think it was in the evenings. That was after 1921, after the minister came.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Was, was there, when you were growing up, was there a minister that lived there?

speaker: Hattie Wilken Johnson
words: Yes, oh, they lived up there. He lived right there. He's Reverend Finke.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: This was when you were growing up?

speaker: Hattie Wilken Johnson
words: When I was growing up.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: He was German, and my, you know, Martha, the weapon down there?

speaker: Hattie Wilken Johnson
words: She was a Finke girl. She's just my age, she... And, and, Reverend Finke was her father. They had four girls, and then had two boys. That is, Martha's mother passed away and he got married again to his, to her mother's sister, Reverend Finke did. And she was up there. And then he, she passed away later too when Martha was about, oh, 17 or 18, I think. Do you remember him, Reverend Finke, from when you were... Oh my, yes, he was there then until 1920.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: What was he like? What kind of a minister, what kind of person was he?

speaker: Hattie Wilken Johnson
words: He was, I've got some pictures, I haven't got them here, but, he was a heavyset, a big mustache. He, he drove the horse, he drove the daffy Juliet on Sunday afternoons at Cameron in the mornings. He'd go down, and preach down there in the afternoons. Yes, he was, oh, I'd say not just overly fat or anything, but a heavyset man. And he's full -headed, kind of full -headed, but he had a great big mustache.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Did he farm too?

speaker: Hattie Wilken Johnson
words: No.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Just minister?

speaker: Hattie Wilken Johnson
words: What he did was he was a minister there. And what he got later on, he had a place back up in the timber where they would go up to. But, the congregation, they'd bring vegetables and all those things there, you know, gifts and the... They came from the, you know, from the farm, they'd take that there to the minister. And of course he was paid too, for his yearly payment. I don't remember what it was, I would, of course I was swamped, I didn't pay too much attention to that.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Did he have much, did he do much in the community as far as taking, taking care of people's problems and that kind of thing? Did people come to him for help?

speaker: Hattie Wilken Johnson
words: Well, I told him if they came to him, if they came up there to him, but he didn't go around any other way, like some of the other ministers did. They'd go up to his place, he had a study up there, he had lots of books there in the front room. At the parsonage or... At the parsonage, yes. And when I was going to, we had to go to Bible school, that was a little, you know, it was often that there was a first church, you know. And we had to go three months from New Year's until Bob's Sunday, that was the Sunday before Easter, where we were confirmed. We had to go two years and study the Bible. And then later on, boy, then they didn't have it, they could learn enough, you know, without going there. But that sure cut into our school year. Those three months when they only had six or seven months or eight months of school, why, there wasn't much time left from when we started, you know, from October or sometimes later on was the September.

speaker: Hattie Wilken Johnson
words: And, but we had to study and we did study.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: How often did you have to go to the Bible school?

speaker: Hattie Wilken Johnson
words: Oh, that Bible school, that was every day, that was four days a week for three months.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: What months of the year would it be?

speaker: Hattie Wilken Johnson
words: January, February, March, and then until Palm Sunday, that would be the first Sunday before Easter.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: But did you go to school at the same time?

speaker: Hattie Wilken Johnson
words: No, we couldn't go because we'd have to go to that school. Oh. And that made it just awful hard. There's one of my girlfriends that went to the school, she didn't go to Bible school, she didn't, they didn't go to church up there. But she'd bring the lessons down to me, so I just kept up with that. All of the, you know, took my old geography and those that, she'd tell me the lessons. And I studied that at home too and went to the eighth grade. I came down here to Kendrick and I started the ninth grade, so I didn't learn anything, all except grammar and then Latin. Of course we didn't have any Latin and nobody else needed to learn that. It's valid you hadn't had that for years, but Latin, of course, that was really hard for me.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: You went right into the ninth grade down here from the eighth grade up there?

speaker: Hattie Wilken Johnson
words: And they didn't say any difference, you know, we had, I gradually jumped there and I come down and I went right along with the other ones.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: How old were you when you started the Bible school?

speaker: Hattie Wilken Johnson
words: Well, that was for two years before, that was when I was twelve and thirteen and then I was fourteen when I was confirmed. I was fourteen just the week before I was confirmed. It sounds like the religion was pretty strict, it was pretty important up there. Yes it was. And then I went down here to Kendrick for those months in September until New Years and then of course I had to finish out my Bible school up there. I made my grades down here, all of them, but I had to study my Latin. I never did learn it. And the grammar, I had to learn that, but he was kind of nice on that. And of course the higher grade, well that made a difference too, but all the other ones, history, reading, writing, all those, I just went right along with those.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Would you say that most of the people at Cameron who belonged to the church were very serious about religion?

speaker: Hattie Wilken Johnson
words: They were.

speaker: Hattie Wilken Johnson
words: They were in those days. There wasn't any time on a Sunday that they weren't in church, but sometimes of course maybe if somebody was sick or something, mother would say, oh mother didn't go every Sunday because she was a lot of the time she didn't feel that all the rest of us went. And we could, because of the mud, get too deep or something, a sled while we went up the sled with the team.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Did they have revivals up there too or wasn't that a part of the church?

speaker: Hattie Wilken Johnson
words: They revived them when they were born at that time, I think. I shouldn't say something like that. Well if that was their, their different denominations have different beliefs. I don't really know what that is. Oh I know there's so many different beliefs when you go to thinking about it. I have my nephew, he's down in Utah now, and he's writing a history of the whole family and his wife's family and all. It just happened that my husband was born in Oregon and Ben's wife's folks, they were just kind of related back there. We've kind of come together and that's so they're taking us from there.

speaker: Hattie Wilken Johnson
words: Oh there's so much to do sometimes my head just whirls around, I have to think of other things. Well I just, I am curious about, because it's the only German congregation that I know of right around in this area, I am curious about the... Well they did, they were very serious with the church and of course they started the church at first.

speaker: Hattie Wilken Johnson
words: Now up at Genesee they have the church up there, Genesee or Uniontown.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: I guess they're not far apart, are they?

speaker: Hattie Wilken Johnson
words: I've heard lots about them and I've gone through there but then I just don't know so much about it. The minister would come from over there at Genesee and he was what they call those...

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Circuit writer?

speaker: Hattie Wilken Johnson
words: Circuit writer. And he'd come there and he'd have a sermon for the once a week at the old schoolhouse and he'd stop at the people around. But that wasn't very often and then when enough of the Germans were up there they came together and they voted then on the church that was in 1989 or 1990. And they formed a church up there and they called for a minister and there was a minister, Groeschut was his name. He'd just come from Germany, he and his wife. And Spokane, he came there, he was there four years. And when he left he went to Spokane and he had one of those big churches up there, he and his wife. His wife had twins up there, Cameron, they lived there those four years. Of course his wife had come from Germany where they were higher up, you know, had servants and everything. And she was just, she didn't know enough to put wood in the stove when it was cold. All those things, she'd dress in her fur coat and all and somebody would come by and she wanted to bring the wood in and put it in because she was getting cold. I remember them talking about it, of course I wasn't there but then that was before I was born too.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: So she wasn't ready for pioneer life?

speaker: Hattie Wilken Johnson
words: Oh, not at all, not at all.

speaker: Hattie Wilken Johnson
words: And I don't know whether Reverend Groeschut was too much or not but they went to Spokane and they had a lovely church up there later on. And she just done very well up there. And then after he left there was a, oh Reverend, oh I should know, they baptized me anyway just after I was born. And his wife was English American, she didn't know much German, she was there about two years. Then after that Reverend Groeschut came and he was there from 1898 up until 1904. And then after that Reverend Finke came and he was there until he was left in 1920.

speaker: Hattie Wilken Johnson
words: Do you think that his daughter, Mrs.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Wilkin, would remember?

speaker: Hattie Wilken Johnson
words: I should probably talk to her sometime about him. Well, yes, she could tell you all about Reverend Finke and about that because she's the one that would know. Because they came, they'd been there for a little spell in 1898 or 1990, just for a few months. And then they went to another hostage and then this help came. And then they came back in 1904 and she could tell you from then on, she could tell you just so much more about it.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: At home, did you speak German or English? Oh, at home we talked about punnage, you know what punnage is? What is that?

speaker: Hattie Wilken Johnson
words: Well, that's...

speaker: Hattie Wilken Johnson
words: Mother never spoke that she always spoke high German. But if you know anything about it, I went to see her when he formed the church. He went through Germany and picked out the best words, you know, and that's where high German comes from. And the others, they were just like, right here in the United States, you go down there, they talk. Oh, in the south and in the east. The dialects.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: What do they call that?

speaker: Hattie Wilken Johnson
words: Well, I call it dialects. dialects, yes. Well, that's what the punnage is.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: How do you say that now?

speaker: Hattie Wilken Johnson
words: Pudnage. Pudnage. But that's flat German, I guess. Flat German instead of high German. Yes, and high German. That's what they call it. So you spoke flat German at home. Well, we did later on. But Mother never did until she come out here, out west, and she picked up the English language because there were all the people down here, Kendra, you know, somebody else, she could talk that. But there were some of them that, the women, they never did talk anything but the German. They never did learn to talk English or they didn't want to. They could probably understand it. But Mother did write for them again. It was one of the neighbors up there that she was English. And of course Mother chummed with her too and visited with her, that is.

speaker: Hattie Wilken Johnson
words: They go back and forth, they didn't chum much but, you know, visit back and forth.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Well, did most people, did most of the people speak Pudnage rather than high German?

speaker: Hattie Wilken Johnson
words: Well, they were at home, yes. But you go to church one a year, you spoke the high German.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Was there a lot of difference between the two?

speaker: Hattie Wilken Johnson
words: Well, there was quite a bit. It was just like, and all the different ones that talked Pudnage too, they didn't speak the same language. But there were some of them, they had a kind of itch in there when they were talking, you know, itch, itch, itch. We always called them the itches. Of course we couldn't understand them hardly either. But they were German, but then they went to church and all and they talked the high German.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Were they from a different part of Germany?

speaker: Hattie Wilken Johnson
words: Well, I think they came from Minnesota or some place. I don't know where they came from. But I think they must have, they probably come from a different place of Germany. My folks came from Moldenberg to Germany. And that is what my father did. But then there's, you know, it's just all those little, well like the little states here, you know, or towns and everything.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: When did you start speaking English?

speaker: Hattie Wilken Johnson
words: Oh, just when I started talking to her about this, I started to talk to the English team because my older brothers and all of them, they were talking English. You see, I had those older brothers that came from back there. There were some of them. Well, they always talked, talked to English. And I learned that just as quick as I learned the others. Of course I learned German at home to talk there. But we did, wasn't very, and that pretty soon it was mixed up when father and brother, they talked in English, words that mix in with it, you know. We didn't have any sense. And then the neighbors down there, they were English too. They'd come up, about the same age as my brothers, from the girls about my age and so on. They'd come up one day for supper. You'd have a whole big long table there. And then another time we'd all be down there. Of course, bean soup, a big kettle of bean soup and beans and maybe milk soup that was red and milky and all that. But there was nothing. They had bench along there at the boys' set.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Was there a lot of visiting back and forth?

speaker: Hattie Wilken Johnson
words: Well, among us there was. That is, among the Speckers and our folks right there. But the other ones too, they visited on Sundays and after church and all. But the Speckers, when they lived there, why, it was just down over the hill from where we lived.

speaker: Hattie Wilken Johnson
words: It was just back and forth, you know. Was it mostly other, but it was mostly other German families.

speaker: Hattie Wilken Johnson
words: The Speckers, they could talk, but they were English. And so, Specker, well he came from a different part of Germany. He was the county assessor for years and years, when they first started that to go. And then when he passed away, later on one of his boys was the assessor. And they talked English quite a bit, you know, the Speckers did. And of course he'd have to, you know. And we learned that just as quick as of course going to school to English school.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: When you kids were at school, did you speak among yourselves, not in a classroom, but when you were playing outside, did you speak English or German?

speaker: Hattie Wilken Johnson
words: Oh, it was always English, unless we were at German school, and then it was English too.

speaker: Hattie Wilken Johnson
words: For us younger ones. Now I don't know what my older brothers, I don't think they, they probably spoke, but they didn't. They always spoke English.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Were you supposed to talk German at the church school?

speaker: Hattie Wilken Johnson
words: Well, they were at school, yes, we were supposed to. But you didn't. We'd go out on the coast or something like that and it was English. It turned into English by that time. But my brothers, they always spoke English.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: What were some of the old German customs that the families kept out here?

speaker: Hattie Wilken Johnson
words: Oh, my folks, I really, I probably had a butcher lot. My father always sold, Robert from the beginning, he's the first one that raised these beans. You know those white beans up there, they lived for a while down to halfway down before they could move into the house, had the house finished on our place. And then first father, he'd build the barns and everything and to cultivate the land and he'd go back and forth. They had raised about five acres of those beans, the white navy beans. And the miners would go through, would go to Pierce, they'd stop and they'd buy that. And father, of course, the butcher, they'd always take the bacon and once in a while a half, it was always the bacon. He sold all that to them. Then some of the men down, the harvest maker down here, all they had to have, Mr. Wilkins' hams, even after they lived away from there, they always sat in for them, two of them for a year. One of them was down at Pelham and the other one was over at Tacoma. They always butchered quite a few hogs, of course there were quite a few in the family and we had lard and everything. We had smoked meat and smoked sausage, all those good things.

speaker: Hattie Wilken Johnson
words: That's where father, he learned talking. English language, you know, right from the beginning, he was after he was out here. He was a commissioner, he'd go down to the courthouse, you know, what they wanted for the roads up there, the portion, you know, and all. I don't know, there was never a time, I don't think we ever talked among ourselves so much about that footage, but that, of course, that moved into it. It was always, you know, English. We went to school, we come from school.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Did he actually work on the roads? Did he do the road work?

speaker: Hattie Wilken Johnson
words: He was a road worker here and he worked on the roads too.

speaker: Hattie Wilken Johnson
words: If he died in the road, we'd have to take the team and go and he had a long ways to go up around Leland and then up around, not quite to south, but around there and then back down again and all those roads up and down around. Of course the roads, they weren't built much so they had to dig the roads out of it. He worked on that one for Gulch too, to dig out a road to go down there, to get down here to Kendrick. Yes, he worked there about 20 years that he was over here. Then, of course, they were elected every year, those road workers years. And then he was getting, you know, tired of that and then there was a Mr. Oldie, he was, he'd been on over here for some time. I don't know, later on they had, you know, it was changed. Right now they have, they come from Los, about the ones that come up here to do the grading and everything and tell them what to do.

speaker: Hattie Wilken Johnson
words: You know, the, I was thinking about the old, some of the old customs, like, you know, things that they had done in the old country and things that they had done that their parents had done. You know, the sorts of things that came from Germany.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: I was wondering what, you know, like, besides going to church, were there holidays that they kept special? They had their holidays and there they always had, in Germany they had the Beerschteibend, you know what that is?

speaker: Hattie Wilken Johnson
words: No. Well, I could tell you that, but that's for the young, the young boys they gathered in the, in the evenings, you know, they'd have, were out in Germany. They'd go, they'd play cards, I suppose, and drink some beer and so on. Just like they do now when they go down to the pool room. That's the way I gathered in the spot.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: How often did they do that? Back in Germany?

speaker: Hattie Wilken Johnson
words: Yeah. Oh, I guess they'd have it every week or maybe twice a week or more than that.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Is that what, is that what they did, did they do that here too?

speaker: Hattie Wilken Johnson
words: No, no. After they'd come out here, I don't know, I don't know what they done in there. But a soldier, of course, I wouldn't know about. And here it was all, they never did. But what they done was we'd go to the neighbors, you know, play cards.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Like you were telling me, like you were?

speaker: Hattie Wilken Johnson
words: No, they didn't have any, any special. I guess down here where they had the hotels when they were building the railroad, they had hotels, you know, that was where they, they had worked. And they usually had a saloon down here too. I remember one of them was a Terry, that name Terry. He had a big story called it. I always remember that. They had a family.

speaker: Hattie Wilken Johnson
words: But they were nice people otherwise, I guess. But I was too small too. He kept a saloon. He kept a saloon down here.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: What about the food? Was there, was there the cooking like they'd done it in the old country?

speaker: Hattie Wilken Johnson
words: Not so much, I don't, that I know of. Well, they knew how we used to cook beans or soup or something like that. I don't, I really don't know how they cooked much over there, but I think it was probably on that same order.

speaker: Hattie Wilken Johnson
words: You know, one thing that I had heard about in talking to some people whose parents came from the old country is that the father usually ruled the house with an iron hand and the mother, the wife, kind of took orders from him.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Was that how it was out here too?

speaker: Hattie Wilken Johnson
words: That's the way, I don't know whether it wasn't that way this here, but over there it was that way.

speaker: Hattie Wilken Johnson
words: And I've got some pictures though. I don't know if I packed things in or the other ones. They didn't fire me. These boxes, I haven't looked at them, but there's pictures from Germany. And well, the father told them the barn was right next to the house. They were together. And the women, they had to milk the cows and they fed, of course they, so the potato field is everything there. And the men, they had, of course they had sheep and they done, they herded the sheep out. They're going up and they had to learn to knit. So they wasn't idle, you know, when they were herding sheep. They had wool and yarn, they'd spin the yarn and then they'd, they'd get their stockings and all that. That's what the boys had to do.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: The boys did that?

speaker: Hattie Wilken Johnson
words: The boys had to do that when they were growing up. They were just boys and the girls, they had to milk the cows and do those things and help in the house to cook and all those.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Well, what about in your family? Did your father tell your mother what to do?

speaker: Hattie Wilken Johnson
words: No, I don't think so. Not after I was growing up. No, she'd done her work and he'd done his work. She'd tell him what to do. And he'd tell us kids what to do. And that I could, you know, looking back, she raised lots of geese and turkeys and all those things. You know, we still lived in the garden before they could get their new house built. And I remember I was two years old and I'd go out there and they'd take after me, those gobblers. And I couldn't go out there again. And Father, he just bought a herd of hive of bees before that. And they were out back at the garden. And I could go out there, the dog, and I was just a little like a dog. He'd go and hide the bees then. At Ojai and those bees. And he'd bring me some candy, you know, put a little bit in the bags, you know, each bag. If they had little bags, I'd always give them back. And he'd just put a couple pieces in. I'd take them over into the beehive. I just remember going there. Then the bees would come out and they'd come around the honey. And oh, how I'd laugh and prayed with the bees. I never got stung until after I'd done that for quite a while when the fruit trees were blooming. And my sister and my older brothers, they made wreaths around my head. And of course when I got there, they didn't want me to go. They was going to take me in the house to the folks at C .D. I got away and I had to run to the bees so they'd see me and I knocked on the air and I got stung that time.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: You knocked? What did you knock? Huh? You said you knocked?

speaker: Hattie Wilken Johnson
words: I went there to the, I went and ran over to the beehive. Yeah. And I wanted Dan to see me and my apple blossoms and whatever blossoms he brought out of the orchard. And of course they wanted some of that honey, you know, for those. And Mother came out from the house and Father and of course she got after the kids and I run to her mother and she had a big apron on and put that over me. And of course after I got over there I went for the bees. But that didn't stop me from taking care of my bees. I was out to the next day again.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: What did you do to take care of the bees? What did you do?

speaker: Hattie Wilken Johnson
words: Oh, Father took care of them.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: You just had fun with them?

speaker: Hattie Wilken Johnson
words: They, yes, they had things to, all the different buckwheat they planted, the buckwheat. And the different things and sweet clover and that Father had around. Some of the neighbors didn't thank him for the sweet clover. Of course they had the other clover too. And that was, they would get that, the honey out of that.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: And out of the orchard too, see that big orchard?

speaker: Hattie Wilken Johnson
words: And all those fruit trees.

speaker: Hattie Wilken Johnson
words: And I hoped, but anyway it was always, but I know that he planted those things for the bees.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: You say the neighbors didn't care for the clover that he planted, the sweet clover?

speaker: Hattie Wilken Johnson
words: No, they didn't want him to plant that. They said it was a weed. Of course he didn't plant it on any of their things. It was just some hour ground ground around there. That's it.

speaker: Hattie Wilken Johnson
words: I think clover is pretty good for the soil.

speaker: Hattie Wilken Johnson
words: Yes, and there's other, that's her clover is too. It's like alfalfa or something like that you plant under. And then the other, quite a few of those things. I wonder why you liked bees so much when you were a child. I couldn't play with anything else. But after that my little brother came along and of course he'd be out there, but I was always out there with the bees. And even as I was growing up I always watched it when they'd swarm and we'd have to catch the queen, you know, to put her in there. I learned how to do all those things.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: You learned how to catch the queen?

speaker: Hattie Wilken Johnson
words: Oh yes, yes I had to learn all those things.

speaker: Hattie Wilken Johnson
words: But that was in the summertime that I always had to help and watch if the bees were going to swarm or anything like that. And if Mother would come out when Father was working on the road we'd have to get out and catch the queen. And I think they'd settle on, they'd bunch them and they'd settle up on a limb or something. And put the queen in the hive and then the other ones we'd check down and they'd go down and they'd go in the hive then after her.

speaker: Hattie Wilken Johnson
words: I'm surprised that as a little kid you didn't get stung a lot when you tried to play with them.

speaker: Hattie Wilken Johnson
words: And I always went barefoot too, you know, we always had, kids always had to go barefoot in the summertime. And some of them now I think go down to this place down here.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Carla Emery's?

speaker: Hattie Wilken Johnson
words: Yes, they all go barefoot. But we didn't go barefoot when we went to school. We didn't. Around the house we always went barefoot in the summer, soon as summer came I realized.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Did you want to or you just didn't want to use your shoes?

speaker: Hattie Wilken Johnson
words: Well we didn't have shoes in the summer. They would get smaller, you know, we wore them out during the wintertime. Well we did get some shoes for Sunday, we had to wear some for Sunday because we'd take a lot. But we all walked barefoot. There were some older ladies there that came from Minnesota. They went barefoot too, I remember they'd come down. The folks, it seemed like looking back, why they had to, the folks had all that fruit and he had a dryer. We had the dried cherries and apples and put them through apples and prunes. We had to dip them in the lye and then in the clean water. Put them out on the seams so they had to be kept a certain way. And then the neighbors, they would come, you know, and it was really near things that they wondered that survived from our orchard. So it was, there was something going on all the time that we had to do. But it was a great life when you look back on it. Well.

speaker: Hattie Wilken Johnson
words: Kids were really expected to take a lot of responsibility it sounds. Yes, just as we got big enough to do that way. We had to do it, you know, like watching the bees that had to sit out there and all of the bees just walked. You know, tell them, tell mother or whoever was at the house, father used to be out on the road that kind of year. Road over here, you know, they was grading the road and different throwing out rocks. You watched them for when they would swarm. When they swarmed, yes. I have to watch for that, you know, see how everything was. And I helped with different things. We had the honey extractor that was in the fall. We'd have to take all the honey, all those supers, you know, and all the things. We had a lot of them that father would fix those combs. We sold the comb honey and then they got the others. We had a, we put those others, those bigger frames in. And we'd get the honey out of that and then straight up it would come out. We'd put that in the crocks that the different ones would bring down. We'd feel that. There was a lot of work doing all that because we had to, it would be where we had a bee house, but we had to do this in the kitchen. It had to be so warm, you know, hold it, all that. Honey extracted so it would go out. And we'd have to go in the back, the north door. I don't remember that. You couldn't come in the south door because the bees were swarming all around you. Huh. Well, honey, it was always, there was never any end to anything. We just, first of all, they didn't go to school doing chores. We didn't milk so much the wintertime because I guess we got the fresher in the spring and we had to separate. We did for a long time, we didn't have to separate. We had, down the cellar, we had the pans and milk in the, kind of a cupboard for the tent, the fire on the side. It looked so cool. And it was cold down in that cellar, just real cold. That's where we put the fruit and everything. It was quite a good place. And it had the floor of the cement. We let it over.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: What were you going to say?

speaker: Hattie Wilken Johnson
words: Oh, and then around the sides there was, it was lower so you'd run in there and it would drill out quite a ways down below. We had that so that the water would go out again right away. But it was always real cold down in that cellar. Going from the outside we had some doors we'd open out and at night you'd open them out too. But down there, the screen doors was in there when you went into the cellar.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: You opened them at night to, why did you open them at night?

speaker: Hattie Wilken Johnson
words: Oh, at night, we'd stay cold for the night. And then I can remember I went down and just given the milk, you know, took the cream off, took the other milk out and I went out to the pigs. At the cellar we made cottage cheese. You know, we'd take that out and the warm would get warm in the kitchen stove and that's where we'd make it. And then the spring father made cheese too, the regular cheddar cheese. We'd have to put that in the press. That's where the milk came the first part of May. You know, it was, it's going to be a lot of milk. And we had everything. You can name anything, we had it, you know, cellar. And then all the canning we done with canned meat. Of course, they had lots of sausage and all those things. They had greater time for me. They killed hoes and sometimes they'd kill up meat for killing them. We canned that for when thrashing time come. And in those years, there were about 36 men. They'd have ten bundle wagons to the machine and they'd have to have the straw. They'd take the straw back, they had the straw bucket, take that back to fill in the engineer and just running it in the fire room to keep that hot fill, keep the belt going, haul it in the grain. I know they said one day, when they were talking for the thrashing week, they had over 1800 sacks. I just think of those big sacks to pile in. It was five o 'clock, right after five o 'clock breakfast. And it was about five thirty, he said he should eat breakfast. And at six o 'clock the whistle rang, it started thrashing. At nine o 'clock it rang again and we had to bring the lunch out to where they were thrashing. And they got through, there was a half an hour that they'd be, or fifteen minutes, I don't know which it was, on each sandwiches and cake or whatever, cookies you could. And then at twelve noon, while they was in there, twelve, one o 'clock the whistle rang again and they were back to thrashing. Four o 'clock, lunch back out there till again, seven o 'clock or seven thirty they come in to eat supper. That's the way the day went. At eleven o 'clock, nearly twelve, we'd cook at the farmer's hold so they didn't have a cookware. Some of them had cook wagons that was different, they didn't thrash each one of ours.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: So you just cooked when they were at your house thrashing?

speaker: Hattie Wilken Johnson
words: Unless the neighbors helped them. Of course we had to get somebody to help too, the girls, you know, wait on tables and wash dishes and get things ready. It was working well. That was a big production. That was a big production, a lot of them coming, it was just a lot of them. And boy we had to work and get, sometimes we had to get some meat from down here from Long's. They'd bring it up to the post office, they'd send it up and bring it around. First they had the meat wagon, we'd get it from them. That is some of the fresh meat, of course we had the hams and all of those other things, the bacon and all that. And we had plenty of eggs and chickens, we'd have, sometimes we'd dress, it had to be five or six chickens for the, or four. Get them dressed in the morning and get them ready for food. When we got through with that we were really tired. That is us women were. And I guess the men they were tired too.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Did the women, did you women know how to work together real well? I mean to get this kind of thing done?

speaker: Hattie Wilken Johnson
words: Yes, yes we'd get some of the girls, the neighboring girls.

speaker: Hattie Wilken Johnson
words: Martha used to come down, part -time she'd get some girl that would help. And then on Sundays before that they didn't work on Sundays. And some of the other neighbors, when they come down we'd make baked cookies. Five or six big crocks, four six gallon crocks full of cookies. So they were baked for when they come. And we were good cookie makers too, I'll guarantee that. And baking cakes, we'd have two great big layer cakes here for supper. And all the pies for dinner, oh they had to have pies. And six, seven pies to feed that many for dinner.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: How many women would work to put that kind of feed on for thirty -six men?

speaker: Hattie Wilken Johnson
words: Oh they were just mother and my older sister was there, she couldn't be so much. And myself and then another girl. And we'd get ready for when they'd come down to wait on tables, put on the clean white apron, over here, to wait on tables. And there were some of them, the sack sewers, there were three sack sewers they'd come. But they'd come, they wanted to come right in the screen door just as quick as they got there. They'd get there and wash out their big tubs around. All them towels around, they're the big towels that they dry them. They'd get there for the wash, they'd wash them to wash. They'd come early, I always hook the screens, and they'd want to get in to get to the table, maybe to get a sewer. They didn't get in before they'd all come, they'd get it come, I'd open the screen and then they'd come flocked around the table.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: How come the sack sewers tried to get in first? Why?

speaker: Hattie Wilken Johnson
words: Oh there were some of them, they just liked to grab, you know, the first things. I thought they were getting something better, but, and maybe some places they did get in. Not yours. When I was up there old enough to look at them, that was when I was about fourteen, fifteen, and sixteen. After that time, and the word quite so many of them, the trash machine.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Did the crew appreciate a good meal?

speaker: Hattie Wilken Johnson
words: Oh yes, yes they really ate, and they had good meals too. They just had, oh we had other vegetables, you know, of all kinds, and cabbage slaw, and all those things out in the garden. And they were thrashing there, but a little bit later, we raised lots of watermills and musk, that was just the garden. Father planted a big garden, of course they always had to be out there putting the seeds in, the little seeds. And they liked to go, as they can, they're those younger fellows, so we told them to, that after supper, why, we put the watermills up there, they'd go down to the cellar, we brought them, put them in there first thing in the morning, so they'd be good and cold. And they could just eat all they wanted to, but not to go down in the patch, you know, to tear around, they'd, you know, destroy it. And they stayed with that, and boy they did eat them. We had to put them out there where it wasn't far from the pig, the orange, and all the, and they went out there, and then the boys just threw the, that old fence, you know.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: The rinds, you mean? The rinds and all that?

speaker: Hattie Wilken Johnson
words: Not the rinds, but they, when you eat the rinds, yes, the rinds, yes, all the rinds, all that, it got down from the pig. They could really, could appreciate because they was cold, you know, it was like they'd been on ice almost.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: How long did they stay at your place when they thrashed?

speaker: Hattie Wilken Johnson
words: Oh, sometimes three or four days.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: How much sleep did you girls get?

speaker: Hattie Wilken Johnson
words: Very little. Sometimes you had some of the boys coming in to buy dishes for us. We'd give you twelve o 'clock before you went to bed, and by four o 'clock he was rolling out. I bet it didn't last too long. Those were the days.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Did you ever worry about getting enough food out for them during the day?

speaker: Hattie Wilken Johnson
words: Oh no, the oat was all there. We baked bread ahead of time. We'd bake quite literally in the morning. Mother usually done baked the bread, got the loaves in, we had to get that in the oven, you know, a certain time, and then the meat in there. But we had just some big range, had the meat, had the bread, the pies had to go in there, everything just like clockwork. Sometimes we had to dress chickens for that too, when we had chickens. Usually we had other meat. Beef roast, seventeen pound beef roast. And one time we sat down and the butcher didn't send up, he had quite a bit of bones. So I was quite aggressive about it, I worked for him. So Mother called up and they'd send it up to Cameron, and we'd have to get somebody to get it from Cameron, and bring it down. I know they were talking over the phone, we had the phone, and I said, Mother she wouldn't have that much meat. And I said, a town would be shorter to have lots of meat on them bones. In fact it was just one bone off the top of the ham, you know, it was just that one bone in there. So it was a lot of meat. It was all meat. And Mother, she got after me, she was going to be looking for telling that, you know, and she told him, she said, I'll make sure that there's some meat on the bones.

speaker: Hattie Wilken Johnson
words: And she felt just, she just thought that was terrible.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Why was it terrible? Why? Because you complained?

speaker: Hattie Wilken Johnson
words: Well, I told her to say that, you know, because the other time, while we didn't have it, which was mostly bones, you know, it was kind of hard. I told her when she called down to get the meat. Oh, I don't know, I've always been kind of... Speaking your mind. Speaking my mind, tell them what to do, I always could tell them what to do. I think that's better than not saying what you think. Well, I told them what I thought. Oh, Mother, that was all right, and she brought it up, we had that nice roast.

speaker: Hattie Wilken Johnson
words: We had, there was always lots of good meat, always plenty of pies.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: There were those threshers, and some of the threshers, they had pie on, put the pies on, right?

speaker: Hattie Wilken Johnson
words: And they, on the table, they'd take two or three pieces, and we had to have those little dishes and put a piece of pie on each plate. That was around there, they of course made more dishwashing. That way each guy only took one piece. One piece was before him, he couldn't take it from the other fella. Yes, well, it was always just fun, whenever the threshers came, the threshers, it was so much better when they were gone. We still had things to eat, they'd pull up the machine and go, and give the big blow right around the tooth and away they were to the next person. And they'd be threshed.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: It was better when they were gone?

speaker: Hattie Wilken Johnson
words: Well, no, I was glad when they'd go, because we was just hired out, you know. After they were gone, we kind of took it easy as women folks. We had everything, there's leftovers, you know, we didn't have much to cook.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Was that much of a chance for young people to meet each other? Like young, you know, boys and girls to meet?

speaker: Hattie Wilken Johnson
words: The boys and girls were coming there, of course we knew most of them, some of them they'd come down from further away, they'd come down here. Earn some money up to the cannabis and back up in there, they didn't have as much, you know, like the big farmers there. No, they didn't pay much, of course the boys, they'd come in and have dry dishes, some of the young boys our age would just take dry dishes for us in the evenings. No, I don't think, of course I never paid much attention to the boys, I didn't think anything about the boys as boys, I had brothers. I never, I never think about going to dances or anything like that until I was about nineteen. But I just didn't care about it, or going with a fellow, you know, some of the other ones they were wanting to go with a fellow and all. I had brothers that I worked out to, that are my sisters, brothers, some of them lived down at Walla Walla and I worked, you know, at the house, you know, some of the people that could hire the maids, you know, I learned to cook and of course...

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: You went to Walla Walla to work?

speaker: Hattie Wilken Johnson
words: I went down there in the winter time, you know, after I'd through high school up here and went down there. And I was up to Spokane, we went up there with one of the other girls in Cameron, we went up there and were working up there when the World War I started. That was in 1917, we worked there. And when I was home to help, I had to help my mother sometimes, be sick to be hurt, I'd have to come home. Then after I left there, I went to Portland, one of the girls down there was working and I went down there and I worked down there two years. So I come back up and my mother was in the hospital, she had an operation, quite a serious operation, and I had to come up, shake all stones. It was Gritman in Moscow and I went up there, I had to go up a time or two before she could come home. She was in the hospital six weeks and I had to take care of her and he told me just what to do and the head nurse up there, she was Mrs.

speaker: Hattie Wilken Johnson
words: Gritman, and I had to have that tape in her nurse side, you know, and I had to take care of all of that. And then after that I stayed at home for quite a while and after, he wanted me to come up there to be nursing, you know, but after I was at Cameron, and then we went to the three, but there was different minister up there and everything. That's when I got married. After that, I should have did none of that, but I did.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: You what?

speaker: Hattie Wilken Johnson
words: I got married.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: No, but you said you didn't what? You shouldn't have?

speaker: Hattie Wilken Johnson
words: But I had, I should have told you. So I didn't, I went to go up there and be a nurse, but I went to get, I got another fellow down here, the great. Martha married my sister, my brother. That's Martha Wilkins down here. She married her brother. Just the same year, I was married in February. The last February, Martha and George were married in June.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Where did you, you met him at Cameron? Your husband?

speaker: Hattie Wilken Johnson
words: Yes, at the dance up there. It was after I had to get from, back in Portland.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Was he local? Had he grown up there?

speaker: Hattie Wilken Johnson
words: No, he grew up in Pank. He's a pioneer there of Pank.

speaker: Hattie Wilken Johnson
words: Then, down here, do you know any of the, I found American Ridge, Frank Benscoter.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Did you know the Benscoter?

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Jimmy Kaler, did you happen to go there?

speaker: Hattie Wilken Johnson
words: No. She was Benscoter. And we were friends here, and I got over there, and we were friends with the friends ever since they come here to see me, Jimmy and Harry. They've been here, oh ever, for once a month or a couple months, they come over here. They were still just real good friends, the Benscoters, I don't know, all this time. He was a blacksmith, and then later on there wasn't so much of that blacksmith work, and he didn't attend any further work.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Your husband?

speaker: Hattie Wilken Johnson
words: Yes. And then later on, that blacksmithing, he had to work, he had to get up and be down there to work at five o 'clock once a time. He had to work at ten o 'clock once a day.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: At ten o 'clock?

speaker: Hattie Wilken Johnson
words: No, I went to Pank.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Oh, at Pank?

speaker: Hattie Wilken Johnson
words: I guess I went to Pank then to live. And then after that, of course blacksmithing, he'd get up when the cars and the trucks came in, hauling grain and all. And then we worked down there, then he worked for the Forest Service, shot horses, but then the summer times, different times. Then during that, it was in 35, there was a place, 34, 35, up on the hill from Peck, just up north of Clearwater, down there. There was a deal, and Mr. Gill passed away. He had come down to Peck to live, and his family. But he had a loan, a federal bank loan on the place, and he hadn't been paid, which they allowed, you know, in the same way with the taxes, for five years. And so I heard about that, and I told him, let's go up and look at it. And so he went up there, and we contacted the Gill's, and they wanted to sell, because there was just a little bit left, or otherwise you'd been sold. You know, they wouldn't have got a cent out of it. And Mrs. Gill was living.
