And then the federal land bank down at Bluffs, and we talked, that fellow's picture was the paper the other day, he's been working with that all this time. And the lawyer down there, and to get that straightened out so we could buy that without it going through the, you know, have to be sold for the probate. And we took on over that. I had, we had enough money to pay for the, all the back taxes, and the back payments on the place. And then the rest of it, we took the rest of the payments, you know, we paid twice a year, we had to pay that. And we got all that, and paid Mrs. Gill. And then from there we started just from scratch. And then it happened that, that when we were coming down, that grade was down quite a grade. And we're going to come over here to Kendra, to camera, to my folks, so we can get some potatoes and things started. And the car ran over the grade. And of course I was on Crutches then until, oh it was almost Christmas, it was the last of November that I went up there to Crutches, but I kept up. And we had a cow, a milk cow, and raised a garden. And I had nephews that came from Walla Walla, one of them was about twelve, and another one was about eight. She was from Spokane, my sister's boy, they were there too. I made them do their own washing on the washboard.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: That accident, when was that, did that happen?

speaker: Hattie Wilken Johnson
words: That happened on the Peck grade, coming down the grade.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: What year was that, did that happen?

speaker: Hattie Wilken Johnson
words: That was in 1935.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: What happened to your husband?

speaker: Hattie Wilken Johnson
words: Well he got out, he had, his shoulder was dislocated.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Of course he didn't think I was hurt, I didn't say anything, but did you?

speaker: Hattie Wilken Johnson
words: A fellow, well he was a military, but it was just a step from him over the ditch and then up. And he'd come running over there and his face was just as white as snow. And he came and he said we have to get out, the car was just awful hot, it got hot, you know, coming down the grade before it turned over. And Will said, well get up. And of course I had my, he was under there, my ribs, and then he helped to lift that up. And Will helped a little bit with that one, of course Will didn't think I was hurt. I got up and of course on your first hurt you can walk. And I walked down, there was a fellow who came and was going to take us up to our fair to the hospital. And there's two of the ladies thinking about it too, they wanted me to stay there. And I lifted my dress just enough for them to see my knee, oh my God, this is... I didn't say anything, but I just went up to it, Will went up and they shed his arm, and of course he was alright, and he went on. And the doctor came in to see what I looked like and thought he fixed me up. And I was on crutches, he could have cast on it, but he couldn't stay, you know, walking in the crutches.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Was that grade off of Central Ridge in Depeche?

speaker: Hattie Wilken Johnson
words: It wasn't off of Central, it was off of Angel Ridge. Angel Ridge, uh huh.

speaker: Hattie Wilken Johnson
words: I cannot say that my niece lived up there.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: She married, oh what are those horses up there?

speaker: Hattie Wilken Johnson
words: Orofino, they did not stay there with her. They had a little girl and a little boy came after we, about a month after I was in there. And I still hear from her husband, he's over in Washington, D .C. He'd go through with all the, the, uh, the Forest Service, you know, he's that way up there.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Before you bought that place, uh, outside of Peck, did you live in Peck?

speaker: Hattie Wilken Johnson
words: Yes, we lived right in Peck. I want to ask you some about working out when you worked in Walla Walla and in Spokane.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Were they rich people that you went to work for?

speaker: Hattie Wilken Johnson
words: Yes, it was all rich people that had maids.

speaker: Hattie Wilken Johnson
words: And then in Spokane, that's what I, I went to, I was working for the maid, as a maid, and then I, I had another friend up there that had been working up there, there's a lot of them went up to Spokane from around here that would be working. And she told me about this hotel that I worked there, and of course I got better wages.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: In Portland?

speaker: Hattie Wilken Johnson
words: In Spokane. In Spokane. And later when I went down to Portland, I went to the Mount Noble Hotel, that was one of the, one of the big, sniffy hotels, you know, for years after the...

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Well, what was it like to work, um, to work as a maid? What kind of situation was that?

speaker: Hattie Wilken Johnson
words: Well, I had to, I had to, 20 rooms, 21 rooms. But I then just skipped to the beds, because baby had a, uh, an aunt to do all the sweeping, all of the carpets, and a window washer. And there was another lady, all she'd ever sit down curtains and put up fresh curtains. And then the Little Jack girls that had the, the, uh, the bathrooms, you know, and all that, they'd compare all that to Little Jack's. They'd come working, they'd do all that, but the girls, we girls, we just made the beds, and seeing that everything was in order.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: This was in Spokane or in Portland?

speaker: Hattie Wilken Johnson
words: No, that was in Portland. That was at the Mount Noble, at the March Hotel. Well, I was thinking, um... That up at Spokane, I worked at the Sillman Hotel, and that was close to the Davenport. But...

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Um, when you were in Portland, uh, what did you, what did you think of living in the city there in Portland?

speaker: Hattie Wilken Johnson
words: I liked it. I had Martha's sister and I, we had a, had room together. And, of course, I got my meals out at the, uh, at the Mount Noble, and I had dinner, and they served, uh, oh, at picnic dinner, just anything you'd want to have. There was coffee and milk, and there was cream on the table, and butter, and biscuits, and all the meats, you know, everything. There was just a long table, just like a thresher table. That's how I got acquainted with the, uh, with so many of them that come from Europe, you know, after the war was over, and Armistice was signed down there. Well, I was there, and the Armistice was signed, and they came from England, they came from Sweden, and they came from Poland, and I got acquainted with lots of them, you know.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Were they just immigrants?

speaker: Hattie Wilken Johnson
words: Immigrants, yes, they come from over there, but they could come, you know, they got free to come. And so, um, the boys came, and the girls came, or maybe a sister or brother came with them. But they come to get away from that war -turned -country, you know, back there. And, of course, I got acquainted with lots of them.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: What would you do?

speaker: Hattie Wilken Johnson
words: Connie, Connie worked there, she'd been in England, and worked for, uh, as a nurse to help, you know, but they, of course, she told us about some of that, those soldiers that had brought in, you know, basket cases and all that. You know, it was kind of gruesome, but, and she worked there, but she wasn't a nurse, but she helped working there. And in this place where she lived was a lady. Castle, and this was a cat out of a castle. It was turned over for the soldiers, and the nurses there. And she could tell all about that. It was Connie, and she came with her brother, came over, and she came to America, too.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: So, most of these people that were immigrants were working at the Multnomah Hotel?

speaker: Hattie Wilken Johnson
words: Yes, yes, just a great many of them.

speaker: Hattie Wilken Johnson
words: And then a great many of them was from around the West, but then a good many of them came in there.

speaker: Hattie Wilken Johnson
words: And they went to work as maids and, uh...

speaker: Hattie Wilken Johnson
words: Maids, and, uh, Connie was working at, uh, let's see, I don't, she wasn't a maid, she'd done some other work. I think she was, uh, over that hallway. You know, they kept that safe and kept it dusted and then gathered up the linens, you know, to take up to the room where they took it down. They had their own laundry there. And then they'd bring it down, they'd bring down the linens, you know, so much to the, to our little pantry where we kept our linens and all those things. When we'd take it off the bed, we'd throw the dirty linen under the, there, so they'd gather the linen, we'd have to count the linen all the time. And some of them worked up there in the linen room where they'd mend and do all those things. Oh, we had a white acquaintance. Then we always, after we had our lunch, we always had a half an hour or two on our own. We'd go up on top of there, I've got a picture of where I'm standing up where the flag, flag is, you know, on the recorder. On top of them. On the stories, about eight stories. And then we'd go and play cards. And the old man that built the hotel, his son was running and she was up in his 80s, but he'd come around and visit. And he just got the most enjoyment out of it, coming and sitting in front of us playing cards. Then later on there's a lady came from Chicago, dancing. They had a dancing room with the epitome. And then he hired her from twelve to twelve thirty. That hour, half hour there to go and we learned to dance, you know, all the waltzes. One step with two steps, three steps, four steps, you know.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: You mean she spent a half hour teaching you how to dance?

speaker: Hattie Wilken Johnson
words: Yes, for a half hour, that half hour. Then she had another girl that come there and was learning to dance over the bars, you know, that kind of dancing. Of course we learned the real dancing, you know, ballroom dancing.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: So would you do that every day for every lunch?

speaker: Hattie Wilken Johnson
words: Well, we'd have that for once a week, I guess for about three weeks or four weeks. I guess we had four weeks. And I don't know after that whether the lady, she was getting more money, you know, or something. But he paid her, you know, for us to learn that. So that's where we learned to dance. And then that fellow, that head man, you know, he was that old fella, he'd have a dance every two weeks for the help, you know, we'd go down there. They came from the fella's one down in the engine room, you know, that heated the whole house and all. That was, you know, we could look at it but nobody could go in there because it had to be kept perfectly clean, you know. What do they call that for the...

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: The boiler room?

speaker: Hattie Wilken Johnson
words: Yes, it had to be real. But he danced too and he was a good dancer. He was taller than I was, a little bit taller. And he could waltz and I could waltz. We were dancing at waltz together. This old man, he'd stand up there and watch us dance, you know, and of course I didn't pay no attention. I was just floating around. And I guess he was too. Anyway, he got dressed up to sit down and play the gynae en encore. He was standing there and we got through and they all clapped and there we were. And he just laughed, he come down and he sat at a little table they had refreshments. And we had old punch and some other dainties, you know, cookies and whatever they have. And he sat there and he just got the biggest kick out of that. This old gentleman.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Would this be in the evening that you would have that?

speaker: Hattie Wilken Johnson
words: Yes, in the evening. About eight o 'clock we'd start and we danced to eleven. That was just for the, you know, just a bonus. But every two weeks they had that in one of the rooms, the dancing rooms.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Did most of the help go to that?

speaker: Hattie Wilken Johnson
words: Yes, well it wasn't all the help. These were the maids and then they were just special ones, you know. And then maybe at another time they had some of the other help, you know, the waitresses or those. I didn't associate with them.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Not with the waitresses?

speaker: Hattie Wilken Johnson
words: Well, the waitresses, they had their own work down on another floor, you know. But of course we couldn't meet because we'd come down and we'd punch in at seven thirty. At the punch of the clock, you know, then drive up at the elevator, go up on the elevator. We'd come out, it was four thirty. We didn't work overtime or come too soon. They didn't want us to come too soon, but sometimes we'd have to come on a street car or something. You'd get there a little bit ahead of time, but you'd just have to wait and not punch in until seven thirty.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Was the pay good?

speaker: Hattie Wilken Johnson
words: It was good pay.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Was it?

speaker: Hattie Wilken Johnson
words: Yes, he paid good. If it was just real good, we could... Martha's sister, Martha came down later, she worked there, but her sister was working as a table waiter. We had a room. It was good. We had good wages. It could save money, you know. We had to learn to go to the Jews.

speaker: Hattie Wilken Johnson
words: Did you know anything about Jews?

speaker: Hattie Wilken Johnson
words: In fact, you know, they sell things cheaper on Monday mornings, you know, because they want to get the first patron in there. If they didn't sell it, well, that was bad luck, but they would think to come down on us. And, of course, Marie, she knew about it. She told me about it. I'd go down if I had Monday off. We had one day a week off. And if I had Monday off, I'd go down to get my clothes or whatever I wanted to get a dress or whatever. I'd get it down there and chew them down. But it didn't take long until the Jews that had been here in America long, come on. They was onto that. They didn't do that. They told me later on that when I was going there, I could go.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Was there any special store that you went to in Portland?

speaker: Hattie Wilken Johnson
words: That was a special store, this Jew. But the other times I'd go to the, to Lippmann Wolf or the other. There was two more big stores down there. Meyer and Frank, I know. Right, yes. And that was a good store. And there was another one, Lippmann Wolf, and then there was another one. You know, I kind of forget, you know, that's been a long time ago. That's been more than 50 years ago.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Did you like, did you find that all the people that you worked with were good people?

speaker: Hattie Wilken Johnson
words: Or were there some people that worked there that weren't, you know, that were...

speaker: Hattie Wilken Johnson
words: All that I met were just real good people. They were just real good people. Some of them went to church, I'd go with them to one church or another. You know, if I happened to have Sunday off, that we didn't. And sometimes in the evening, glad we could go to church.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Were most of them young women? How? Were most of the women young that worked there?

speaker: Hattie Wilken Johnson
words: No, some of them were up quite elderly.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Were some of them married?

speaker: Hattie Wilken Johnson
words: Yes, lots of them were married. Quite a few of them had children, you know, they needed to have a little bit more money to keep going, you know.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Do you remember the wages? How? Do you remember what the wage, what your wage was? Your wage? My age?

speaker: Hattie Wilken Johnson
words: No, your wage.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: How much money you got?

speaker: Hattie Wilken Johnson
words: The wage there was $80 a month. And then it took, of course we had to pay for our room, I said in my head, Martha's sister and I. And then of course we had to go down and buy our bagels, what we didn't have. We had dinner down at the hotel.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Did you have your room in the hotel?

speaker: Hattie Wilken Johnson
words: Not in that hotel we had at another hotel, it was a smaller one. You know, $80 a month doesn't sound like a great deal to me. Oh, no, now it isn't. But then it was, that was top wages, a lot of them they didn't get more than $60 and some of them just $50.

speaker: Hattie Wilken Johnson
words: But that was, of course we had to pay for that. And it took quite a bit, we had to buy our food then for breakfast and evening. But I never ate breakfast, I always just ate an orange or something like that. And then I had the lunch, you know, of course at 11. And then in the evening when we and I, we'd get some donuts, they had their donut trucks, you know, they'd go around, they'd cook the donuts, you know, we'd get them fresh. And we just hiked all over, you know, to see the country there, Portland then. And one park or another, you know, for several hours after we'd come in.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: You would just, you would go to a bakery and buy donuts?

speaker: Hattie Wilken Johnson
words: Well, there was just a kind of a little hole in the side, that's where all they had was making those donuts, those special donuts. Of course we had them in a bag, we'd get, oh, by four or sometimes a few more, you know. And then we'd eat them, you know, while we were walking.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: So you'd spend the evening in the parks maybe?

speaker: Hattie Wilken Johnson
words: Yeah, we'd go and just, not just spend them in the park, but go and, oh, try to see, you know, different parts. We'd go up to a higher place, you know, to look over Portland and see the different things. And after we'd come home, we'd take our, get ready, you know, before we'd go to bed and take our bath and everything and have a sip on a clean dress. And we'd go down to a little hotel or a little restaurant or an eating house and that's where we'd pick up our, we'd have, oh, ham and sandwich and maybe a piece of pie and some coffee and things. The time is over. But we always had to have that sandwich and a piece of pie before we'd go to bed. And we'd go to bed and go to sleep. Then in the morning, I had to go half hour early and shoot to get ready to get down there to function at 7 .30. Oh, it was all fun. We had enjoyed life. It sounds like it.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Did you like working at the Multnomah Hotel better than working as a maid in someone's house?

speaker: Hattie Wilken Johnson
words: Well, it was really better. We had more freedom because we weren't, well, sometimes at the house we had to cook dinner, you know, we had to serve dinner and lunch and there was more, you know. And I had where I, all the places where I worked, they were just real nice, nice homes. Then when I was down there, I had more freedom.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: What was the pay like in someone's home?

speaker: Hattie Wilken Johnson
words: It was 50 or 60 dollars.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: And did you get your room and board too, living in?

speaker: Hattie Wilken Johnson
words: Yes. I had heard when other people have talked to me about work that they did as a maid in someone's house, that sometimes some of the families were a lot more considerate than others.

speaker: Hattie Wilken Johnson
words: Well, I think they were, but I happened to, if I didn't like it very well and I first worked at Walla Walla, so I didn't care so much about it, I soon learned to pick out who I wanted to work for. But some of them weren't considerate, I know that.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: What would the difference be, someone that wasn't considerate compared to somebody that was?

speaker: Hattie Wilken Johnson
words: Yes, they weren't considerate and they, well, they talked like you were another being, you know.

speaker: Hattie Wilken Johnson
words: Like you weren't a human like the rest of the family. Most all of them that I went to, they were just very nice. Had a nice room and then the day off, you know, one day a week, usually the afternoon, I didn't have to cook supper. And then some of the men didn't come home for lunch, you know, they just had a little lunch and sometimes they'd be gone too. They'd go golfing or something. So it, I, that was one time when I had to come home and my mother was sick too and I was working at these folks down at Walla Walla. He was a judge, was a probate judge and he'd gone to Olympia, you know, was one of those pirates. And he'd go hunting for geese, wild geese down there. They were still there, they could go. She went to, she was a great golfer, they'd go golfing much of the time. It was the first real nice I enjoyed working there. When Mother became ill I had to come home.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Were some of these people, were some of these people social climbers? No, I wouldn't, well some of them might have been, but yes, some of it might have been, but they weren't, they hadn't made the, did I tell you what his name was?

speaker: Hattie Wilken Johnson
words: No, no, you said he was a probate judge though. Well, he was a judge and this was Malarkey. And it was Malarkey and another and Malarkey, those three, he was the judge. And then this other fellow, he was, he worked at that too, but he wasn't a brother of them, but this other Malarkey was a brother. And, funny how a person could. Uh huh, can remember. That it was the Malarkey's that, that I liked them just real, real well.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Were there places that you left instead, were there places that you decided you didn't want to work?

speaker: Hattie Wilken Johnson
words: Oh yes, there were some places that I didn't, where they weren't so nice, but of course Miss Malarkey, she was, but at first I think they were the different ones working there, so they hadn't cooked things right or served things right. And I said, well if you just tell me what, what you would have done, why, I could do that, but of course I could every time. They moved while I was there, of course I had a week, two weeks I stayed with my sister while I was moving to a nice new home. And everything was just ship shaped and nice hardwood floors, but there was good many things that we, uh, and they had these rugs, nice oriental rugs. And I had to clean that with a sweeper though, they didn't have any vacuum then yet. But, yes I worked there for over, quite a bit over a year.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Were you expected to do all the housework and cook the meals?

speaker: Hattie Wilken Johnson
words: Well, I had somebody come in, you know, once a month or so that would clean the carpets and things. And then they had the, oh I've done some of the nice washing, you know, her nice table linens and things like that, I've done that and I, the rest of it went to the laundry.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: So what did you do most of the day? For the day?

speaker: Hattie Wilken Johnson
words: Yeah. Oh, I got up and I got breakfast and got that all done and then dusted everything and got the beds made and all that. And when she was there for lunch, why I'd get lunch for her and sit at the table and sometimes it would be someone else coming in to eat lunch too, but not often. And then the afternoon I got ready for dinner, you know, we had the table was set just all, and he served the dinner and I had to pass the plates around. And then his mother would come, she was just way up there too, they were, they had lived there for a long time at, at, at Guadalajara. And everything was just had to be just so that, and then they'd eat their dinner and she had, his mother, they'd have all the family there for dinner and his brother next door there. They'd come over on the Tuesday evenings for dinner. And Monday evenings, his mother would come for the dinner. And then they'd go over there, you know, back and forth. But I had one day off that I didn't have to be home at, which was on Thursday, Thursday or Friday that I went down to my sister's or my brother down there.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Isn't it? Did, did they, did the missus tell you just how she wanted things done?

speaker: Hattie Wilken Johnson
words: Well, when I first came there, she told me how she wanted things done and how she wanted things cooked. And she said, I don't like to tell anyone twice. I said, what if she'd tell me anytime, you know, he'd have to go hunting down there for the birds, down there for the geese and the wild ducks down there in Oregon. And of course we'd dress them and then I'd make them or roast them however they wanted them fixed. But outside of that, and she didn't get the roast, that beef roast, you know, that standing beef roast. Everything was just real nice and of course I knew how to make the pies that they, it had to be cream pie or apple pie. Those were the ones that were the favorite. There were some people, they liked lemon pie the best, but that was their favorites for those pies. But I enjoyed, I enjoyed every minute of cooking because I cooked those things. Then when they'd have parties sometimes, about twice a year, they'd have, they'd come in and she had a, a lady come in to help with the, getting it ready. And of course then the Miss Malarkey and the other ones, they'd come out, her friends, you know, they'd come out and get the plates from the kitchen. And bring in there. I didn't have to do any of that, just get things ready, you know, and have it all set in there. And then I had to wash up after they were gone, of course. That must have been a big job. It wasn't a very good job. I had the sink, you know, you could wash in there, scald them and put them up there, they dried themselves.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Did, did she take a personal interest in you?

speaker: Hattie Wilken Johnson
words: Yes, they were, we were good friends. You know, anytime she'd be there alone or something like that. And Mr. Malarkey, I tell you why, he was always just a real good friend.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: If she'd be there alone, what, she would talk to you?

speaker: Hattie Wilken Johnson
words: Yes, well she wasn't there alone much of the time. Yes, she was there, she'd be read or she'd want to know if I'd read this or that in the books or the magazines or things like that. She always told me which were good and all about it. Though I got to read the Malarkey's, they were important, but Sharpshteyn's.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Sharpshteyn's?

speaker: Hattie Wilken Johnson
words: Sharpshteyn's, you've probably seen that. I've heard the name. Yes, Sharpshteyn, it was in German, Sharpshteyn, you know. And he was the one that was that judge and he went for that and his brother was there, his brother. And then his sister, a married man was in Spokane and he was quite, he was an attorney I guess or something. And Martha's sister there worked there while she was going to business college and she worked there evenings and on her afternoon when she came home. And I got acquainted then with his sister, Mr. Sharpshteyn's sister came in to talk to me there. She said that she had heard often of me there and she wanted to meet me too, so I got to know her too.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Well do you think that they looked at you as an equal rather than as being less than them because you were poorer than they were?

speaker: Hattie Wilken Johnson
words: No, I don't think so. I guess maybe my father had as much money as they did after those days when I was working out of course. But they never acted that way. They never acted or looked down on me. I was just one of them. I waited on them with all that.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: What about meals? Did you take your meals after they were finished or how?

speaker: Hattie Wilken Johnson
words: I would eat anytime I'd start to eat, you know, if I was eating the main course while they were eating it and then of course it rained. And then I'd come in and take off the dishes and all the other things and I'd bring in the dessert. And I could eat, you know, I'd always eat my kitchen table. I had a nice table with tablecloths on it.

speaker: Hattie Wilken Johnson
words: I wanted to ask you something about during World War I, I've been told and always like Otto Schupfer was talking to me about this and I've heard from other people too that there was a lot of, there was feeling against German people at that time around here because of the war with Germany.

speaker: Hattie Wilken Johnson
words: Yes, well there was around here. There were quite a few of them that didn't know too much about it and they thought, well these Germans, why they're not anything at all. They was going to take over and the Germans would have to get out of here. And I know some of them, there was one of the bankers down here. He wasn't too good. And then a fellow from Craig, I think he was, Craig up there, Leland, they come around and they bought the war bonds, you know, they want to sell war bonds. And these three men, they come there and the father, you know, talked to father about buying and he's going to make them buy war bonds and things. And he said, well father asked them about how many should they have. I was at home there then, I was listening to all that of course. And they were telling, you know, that he'd have to buy this and that. I brought out our war bonds and I had some too that I had bought. I asked them if they had as many as we had. Well they didn't say anything, we didn't buy any bonds from them. They didn't come back again. I could have a kind of sharp tongue at that time. I was talking to some of those fellows. But they didn't come back again. That banker down here that went with him, of course he was going to take that to his bank and father banked at the other one at that time. But when the banks closed by this Smith while he was over, came to Peck later on, I knew him over there too, Mr. and Mrs. Smith. That the other bank went out of business and had the one bank here. And father took out and helped, you know, that they got that started. So it wouldn't collapse during that time. That was quite a while after World War, when that was in the big 20s.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Well did Smith give German people a bad time then during the war?

speaker: Hattie Wilken Johnson
words: That was during the war that they was wanting to sell those war bonds. But of course we had that and then there was Mr. Craig and then they wanted to try to belittle the Cameron people. And they came up there and they had to free the American flag up there. Had to get there. And some of them came one night. They found some sisters where they pulled down the flag and they broke and tore it.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: The American flag?

speaker: Hattie Wilken Johnson
words: Yes, the American flag. The next morning the teacher that was teaching there, his name was Goretus, he came from over at Garretus or Goretus over at Genesee country. He was teaching there. And he was going with a girl at the time and they were at the school house, of course it was Dar. And he knew all about that and he got a flag and got it up there the next morning again. And the flag was still gone and then of course they put a big piece in the paper about they found a, oh it was a, what are these outfellow pins there. I don't know whether he found it there or some of them said he found it in a chicken house someplace. But these fellows belonged to the lodge. Anyway he had a big piece in the paper about that. But the flag was up there in the next day.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: A new flag?

speaker: Hattie Wilken Johnson
words: A new flag.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Were those people from Leland?

speaker: Hattie Wilken Johnson
words: They were from Leland, just up around Leland. The ones that come up there to pull a flag down, they thought they were doing a big thing that the old Dutchman up there was pulling their flag down or tearing it to pieces.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Oh, you mean they wanted to make it look like the people at Cameron?

speaker: Hattie Wilken Johnson
words: Yes, at Cameron were doing that.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Was this at the school?

speaker: Hattie Wilken Johnson
words: It was at the school house. But of course the flag was up there blowing the next morning and it didn't. Of course they ribbed them, people just really didn't do any more of that kind of stuff up there.

speaker: Hattie Wilken Johnson
words: I had heard that they didn't want people to speak German during the war.

speaker: Hattie Wilken Johnson
words: No, they didn't want to. The English ones didn't. They were just, the Germans they went on just the same. That's when they started having German, English once a month at the German church. That went on, of course, they had the German and the English church part of the time.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: They had English one Sunday and then German the others?

speaker: Hattie Wilken Johnson
words: Yes, and then after 1921 when a different minister came, by then they had the English church more so than they did in the, then they had German. And then as time went on, then they didn't have the German language. Of course there weren't that many Germans over there. At that first before then, the women sat on one side of the aisle, then on the other side. And after that, why they mingled with all the families they sat together.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Well, did they start using English in the church because of this criticism?

speaker: Hattie Wilken Johnson
words: No, no they just, it's not because there's more that, the young folks, you know, they were, they were talking English all the time anyway. No, it wasn't anymore for the criticism then after the World War.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: So it did, so people kept on speaking German just the same?

speaker: Hattie Wilken Johnson
words: Yes, the Germans get together and they still talk, the police talk, the police talk. Hochdeutschliche, haulem, Hochdeutschliche. It's kind of, it gets me to speak it myself.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Some people spoke high German to each other?

speaker: Hattie Wilken Johnson
words: Oh yes, yes, some of them did. They go to church now, wherever.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Well, would they speak it at home too, high German?

speaker: Hattie Wilken Johnson
words: Well, they would at times, but no, the rest of the time, well, it just depends on some of them. They talked one kind of, of Peddish and the other ones they talked to, another kind of low German, you know.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Well, do you think they ever came to blows over that during the war when people would give them a hard time?

speaker: Hattie Wilken Johnson
words: No, they never did. The Germans, they just didn't pay any attention and they just said, oh, I'm going to go to the hospital, they don't go any better.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: You don't think, do you think that any of the people were afraid that some of these people might really do something to them?

speaker: Hattie Wilken Johnson
words: No, I don't think they did. I think they'd be afraid to do anything. There was too many Germans around there. I think they'd be afraid to do anything else than not putting the flag there or doing something like that. And then during the war, you know, they had to make things for the soldiers, you know, the sewing and everything like that. We had our club there at Cameron and then take it down to Lus and at Leland. They wanted us to come down there, you know, and the ones there. And one lady, she came there, she'd go to Leland and she came there to Cameron too. Why she said it's so peaceful up here, everybody's just working, you go down there, there's no quarreling or anything. And the rest of us, there's nothing to quarrel over. We'd go up in the morning, it's about nine o 'clock. And the older women would be knitting stockings and the other ones we made those stockings, you know, that they'd pull on. And they'd knit a lot of those stockings and things like that. And we'd make the pajamas and all those things, we'd sew that on the machine so the girls would take one thing from the others and baste them. And we'd own some of them, we'd be cutting out and we'd work down there until after we ate lunch up there. And then we'd all go home, the different ones.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Where would you work? Did you go to Leland to work?

speaker: Hattie Wilken Johnson
words: Oh no, at Cameron. At Cameron. At Cameron at the school there. That's where they met the Luther League.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Was it a church group that was doing it or was it the Red Cross?

speaker: Hattie Wilken Johnson
words: It was the Red Cross that was doing that. But we were all working for the community. And then we went to, they asked us, we had the president and the vice president and then the treasurer and the secretary.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: The secretary?

speaker: Hattie Wilken Johnson
words: Yes. There were the four of us and we went down there when they met down at Blauston. And we met down there in the evenings, we had three days. The evening after four dinner for all of us sat around the table. And then they took us, the businessmen down there, they took us for rides. That was when the road up towards Genesee, that was when it was first opened. And it was just a short time before that they could go up there. He took us up that road, you know, that was wonderful to drive up there and then look over the town, coming back. And then he took us up to his house, that was Mr. Beach. You know, his white house up there in the...

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: In Lewiston?

speaker: Hattie Wilken Johnson
words: Down in Kendrick, I mean, at Blauston. You go up 9th Street, I think it is, and it's right at the top of the hill.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: He was the head of the Red Cross?

speaker: Hattie Wilken Johnson
words: Well, he was working with it. Yes, he was a banker down there, he was working with it. And we visited around at some of those other high up places there, the families alone.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: What was your part in the organization?

speaker: Hattie Wilken Johnson
words: I was the secretary. And Helen Milken was the treasurer. And Martha was the vice president, I guess.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: What did I say?

speaker: Hattie Wilken Johnson
words: What was the... And president. Vice president, president. Yes, and the president was... Helen Milken was the president. Martha was... Mrs. Blum, she was the treasurer and Martha was the secretary.

speaker: Hattie Wilken Johnson
words: Well, you said that the person would come up to Cameron and say that people were really working well.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Better than, you think he was saying that you were working better than they were in Leland?

speaker: Hattie Wilken Johnson
words: Well, they would get together and just, you know, correlate.

speaker: Hattie Wilken Johnson
words: They didn't say up here why each one done their own work. And some of the older ladies, oh, they were up in their 70s, maybe up to 80s, some of those 80s that didn't stop them. And so they sewed all the buttons on those pajamas and things like that, you know, that couldn't do the other sewing. And Helen Milken, how we done the cutting out, we cut out all those things and put it together and they sewed it then on the machine. And some of them, they sewed it on the sewing machine, you know, the devil's blind stitches. And it all just went like clockwork. We all just worked together. We didn't, we didn't... And we could laugh and talk too, but we were all serious, all working. And then after we ate lunch there together, then we'd go home.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: How often would people get together to do that?

speaker: Hattie Wilken Johnson
words: Usually every week. Once a week that we'd go.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Well, besides socks, what did they make?

speaker: Hattie Wilken Johnson
words: Oh, boy. They knit those socks and, oh, I don't know, they made those clothes and there was other things we had to sew to fix. Maybe it was just every two weeks that we met. But I know we went down to, to, to Lus in that time. We stayed down the separate days and after that we got the prize, the first prize for all the communities down there because we turned in more things that knew how to do it.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Really?

speaker: Hattie Wilken Johnson
words: And, well, we had to learn all those things, how to do this and that. And then later on after the war was along, before the devil, we didn't do so much of it. I guess the war, we went to Spokane when they, the armistice was signed. It was just, we went up there, by the first of March we went up there to work, Martha and I. And then the armistice was in the later part of March, which was part of April, you know, when they rang the bells and everything.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: You know, I was wondering, like how, how did your, for instance, your father and mother, your father and mother, how did they feel about, about that war?

speaker: Hattie Wilken Johnson
words: That must have been hard because, because— It was hard.

speaker: Hattie Wilken Johnson
words: They had their, their family was over there, not mothers, but father. And, but he knew about it. And then I had a brother right over there from Montana. He joined the, the, oh.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Army? The Army and the Army and the, yes, it was the Army or what was it besides the Army?

speaker: Hattie Wilken Johnson
words: There was the Marines and the— Not the Marines. It was the Army that he, that he joined and went over. And he went over that, that coming fall then, when they started the war and then he was, when the United States went into it. But he went, went over in October, I think. It was one of the first ones that crossed the ship. And there were quite a few from Cameron. Of course, the Cameron and Leland, they had a, they had one of those fellows that, he was a preacher down here that went around and wanted to, wanted to give things. And he was talking about Leland. And there were quite a few of the boys that had to go just right after that. Frank, Frank Schaeffler and just as many from Cameron as there was from Leland around there. And he was talking about there, he said if he could go, he said he'd slit his arm, you know, if he knew he had a little bit of germ blood in him, he'd squeeze that out, the germ blood. And there was those boys, boys along in there. Boy, he was hated.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: He was a preacher?

speaker: Hattie Wilken Johnson
words: He was a preacher. And down here at Kendrick. And later on, why he wanted to go as a preacher, but they didn't send him overseas.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: He was from Kendrick?

speaker: Hattie Wilken Johnson
words: He was at Kendrick, yes. But he come up there, down at Leland, there was lots of them at Leland the same way. You know, they thought the Germans, they just, there's nothing good about them. And the Germans, they never said anything. That became, they came from Germany, they'd been over here, United States citizens all those years. Father, he became a citizen in 1886, shortly, or in 1870, somebody, he came over. And he was here in Idaho, you know, when he got the land. Of course, he'd have to be a citizen to my land. And, but all those years, he'd been a citizen of the United States and here. I don't know, they were, we always said they were such young people that didn't know better. But I know when they got the flag, and that's how they thought that the German church, he said they'd meet up there at the church, and they was doing all that, getting caroms ready and so on. Some of those people, you know, be so ignorant to talk about such things.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: This preacher, did he say what he said in front of people from Cameron?

speaker: Hattie Wilken Johnson
words: About squeezing out his... They were all down there, that was the last, they was having a supper for these boys that had to go. Oh! They had to go, you know, that just within the next day the train went, and some of us girls, we went down there when that train left down at Lusk, and those boys all got on, you know, to... And of course we saw all the bawling about it, and all we... We all had to go down there too, we went down with the car. And some of us went down on the train, and seen the boys off, and the train was there, the people, go back in the deep where they was getting into those cars, and spitting them goodbye. And you know, we didn't have much good news for these fellows that talked about being such good Americans and all. Yes, those were the days that we worked hard for the Red Cross, all during that time, and got back and forth about it. But listen, we knew them as well as anybody did.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: This preacher, he was saying that at the farewell dinner in Kendrick?

speaker: Hattie Wilken Johnson
words: At the camera, at Leeland's. It was at Leeland. But he is a preacher from Kendrick, he preached down here, but he came up to...

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: And a bunch of you were there at Leeland?

speaker: Hattie Wilken Johnson
words: Oh, we were all there, and the boys, they were sitting out on stage, you know. And there was some of them, they believed like this preacher did, they were boys from Leeland, you know, around. But some of them, they were up there that didn't come back again. They went overseas, you know, except in the casket. They came back, Frank Sheffer. He was in the, just before the armistice was signed, the armistice was in November. And just that day, that morning, is when he was shot, he shot his fetal child. And he lived then for several days, a week or so after that. When he was, we got his word, his father passed away as Frank Sheffer. His father passed away. And his funeral was, of course then they had the funeral sometimes on Thanksgiving day in the afternoon. And then the messenger came from Kendrick, the telegram, that Frank had passed away. It came that same day that was all there together. And of course they had, they put their names up, the boys, all of them had a slate there. They went, the ones that didn't come back, had the stars on them. It was on there for quite a while. Of course now they have the new church that isn't there. But they probably could have it someplace up there.

speaker: Hattie Wilken Johnson
words: But I would think that it would be hard to think that I might have to be fighting against maybe my own relative if I had to. Well, they probably, but then they were relatives in a way that they had to, they didn't know them.

speaker: Hattie Wilken Johnson
words: You know. Of course my father's had two brothers left over there, and their children, some of them. Of course they went to war too, but they lost a lot of theirs while they were fighting with France, you know, different ones. They'd lost a lot of them over there too.

speaker: Hattie Wilken Johnson
words: Of course we didn't know any of them. There was a father's younger sister came. After he came to America, she came later in the years, and her husband, they came to Minnesota there and they were buried there. And they lived there, and then later after the war was over, another brother -in -law came. And his sister passed away there, and this brother -in -law came with the two girls and boy, family, they came to Kansas. They lived there. We got acquainted with them. They came out to visit us. The two girls did at different times. So I don't know too many of them.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Did most of the boys that went overseas see active duty fighting the war?

speaker: Hattie Wilken Johnson
words: All of them did.

speaker: Hattie Wilken Johnson
words: My brother, he went over there and then later on he went under Hoover, you know, he was passing out food, you know, things. He worked with him for a year before he came back. He didn't come back until 1919 almost in the fall. It was, I guess, September when he came home.

speaker: Hattie Wilken Johnson
words: And then of course he had the post office up at Cameron. After that I went down to, that's when I went down to Portland to work, in 1920. Or 1920, you know, like after Christmas. I say, over Christmas I went down there. But, well, I waited quite a bit, you know. I liked to go to work someplace where I could earn more money than I could, you know, right close around here. And then the times that I came home mostly after I was older was when my mother would be sick or something and she had an operation this last time. And of course I took care of her for those months. My youngest brother was home then. And of course we, after she's able to sit up and, you know, be able to get around. It took till spring before she was able to get up and walk, not be up, up in a chair. And then we papered the house and then it all failed when farming started. Of course we went ahead with the work I helped with my brother. You know, he worked, and then the milking and things like that. And then he went to church. Of course, Father, he did what he had to do. We always worked when we was at home. And went to dances later on when I got so that I didn't go after I started going to dances. But then I went to dances.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Were people against dancing at Cameron when you were growing up?

speaker: Hattie Wilken Johnson
words: No, we had dances up there. The young people, they always danced. You know, Germans go ahead and dance. Learn to have that. And they had, my brother, my brother played the accordion. The other one, Bill Milkey, he played the violin. And, let's see, there's another one, he played the horn. And it was just a lot of fun. Dancing and playing.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: What kind of music did they play?

speaker: Hattie Wilken Johnson
words: Oh, just all the, they had waltzes and one step, two step. Sometimes four dances. They'd have to try to get some girls to dance, you know. They'd go around and they didn't try to get the girls off of their feet.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Where would they hold the dances?

speaker: Hattie Wilken Johnson
words: They left the hall there at Cameron. There was kind of a hall there. It used to be the post office and everything. They had to move the post office to a different place, they called that the hall. And they could dance there. They kept the four pretty selected seats around the side. Sometimes there'd be people come in, these medicine men.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Have you seen those?

speaker: Hattie Wilken Johnson
words: I've heard of them.

speaker: Hattie Wilken Johnson
words: They'd come in, you know, peddle their wares and give a show and so on. But after that was over with, we had dances there. Usually it was kind of towards spring where they'd have something like that.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: How'd they keep the floor slick?

speaker: Hattie Wilken Johnson
words: Oh, they kept it slick whenever they put that down where they, I don't know, the floor was a little bit rough, didn't make any difference either. You could still dance.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Did these guys sell medicine when they put on the show?

speaker: Hattie Wilken Johnson
words: They had those, those, well, they're skits, you know, when they're selling that medicine and everything. Well, they packed up by then. Sometimes they'd stay and dance too, but usually they'd go on. Sometimes they'd have some place to stay, you know, where they could...

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Did people buy the medicine?

speaker: Hattie Wilken Johnson
words: Oh, some of them did. Some of them, if they had the kind of medicine that would go down, they could feel good.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: What was it?

speaker: Hattie Wilken Johnson
words: They had several things. There was medicines, you know, that was prohibition at that time.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: So they were alcoholic?

speaker: Hattie Wilken Johnson
words: Alcoholic, and then some of the other ones too. That man, you know, at Cameron, they'd keep some of those. Alconcrier, that was a medicine too, you know, they're supposed to, then a month and stuff like that. But they'd sell it for a drink too. Well, they could always get plenty of that stuff, I guess. But they never, not any kind, but sometimes they'd have them outside, you know.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Was there much moonshine made around, do you think, around Cameron?

speaker: Hattie Wilken Johnson
words: Not just many, not any moonshine. I never heard of anybody making moonshine. Oh, some of us made wine. We always made wine at home. At Jerry's or all that stuff, we always made them, made them a bunch of wine. I even made some dandelion wine. A bit of crock, I think. And it was good. I picked my dandelion, I followed that, it was an old cookbook. And I walked, cleared out of Leland to get some lemons and orange, you know, that I would eat in there. And I leave that, and I had that crock, you know, to straighten it all out. And they'd have to, after an age or while, and I bottled it up, put it in a cellar. One of my brothers, he liked that too well, and I got down on my head. Of course, I gave it to father and mother. I forgot about it then. I was working out on the wintertime, but I didn't come back. And father was talking to some man that, he said somebody stopped there, visited with father. He'd eat dinner, and he'd get her, father was telling about some wine that I had made. How good that wine was, and I just happened to think, I went down to the cellar. It was up to the wine, I put it to where I'd hid that because I thought kids could find that. And I brought that up, it was called Blame Sausage, get off, and I poured some of that wine for them. And boy, that was good. And I gave mother, son, and all of us, we drank, just a bottle about that high. They all drank that wine. It was good, it was just, I never tasted wine that tasted like that. I don't drink wine, not sure anything. You know, you taste that wine or the cherry wine, it was something that we had, you know.

speaker: Hattie Wilken Johnson
words: I know what you're talking about because I've had a little dandelion wine once, and I thought it was just the best wine I've ever had.

speaker: Hattie Wilken Johnson
words: Well, it's a kind of a, oh, a light color, like a damp light, you know.

speaker: Hattie Wilken Johnson
words: And that was good. I liked it myself. So people didn't care about that, whether it was Prohibition or not, they'd still make wine.

speaker: Hattie Wilken Johnson
words: Yes, they still made it, yes. That's when we made that, but they never bought it, your father never brought anything.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: What about beer? Didn't a lot of people like to drink beer?

speaker: Hattie Wilken Johnson
words: Yes, the Germans, they usually always got a keg of beer or two, and they had a party or something like that.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Well, how did they get that during Prohibition?

speaker: Hattie Wilken Johnson
words: That was before Prohibition started. And then during Prohibition, I don't remember any of them having beer. I don't remember at all that any of them had beer during Prohibition.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Did your folks, when the war started, the first war started, do you know whether they thought it was going to go as long as it did? Did what?

speaker: Hattie Wilken Johnson
words: You know, that first World War, it was long. It started in 1914, and it didn't, it went forward.

speaker: Hattie Wilken Johnson
words: Yes, and it was overseas, and then over here it was 1917.

speaker: Hattie Wilken Johnson
words: Yeah, 1917, and then it ended in 19... Then that's when the war... And then it ended in 18. Yes. But I was thinking, I wonder what they, I wonder if they just thought it might end really, really quickly, because it went on so long.

speaker: Hattie Wilken Johnson
words: They didn't know, you know, because the war over there, well, of course, they sympathized with their relatives back there, but when the war started here, why, they was with the American, with the troops, you know, they had to go. You never heard of the Germans, you know, like some of the English, or some of the other people, you know, that weren't German. A lot of them, they tried to talk down so they could take the land away from the German people up here and so on, you know. They were real snobbish or snotty that way. It didn't work out that way, but they went right on working. And, of course, they couldn't hear from Germany, but others started taking it up until then. He always took the twice a week spokesman and the Nusseant Tribune, too. But after the war, after the wars came, he took the, this, the Lewis and the daily paper, and the same with Spokane paper, he got all of these. And, of course, he took a lot of the other, they were German papers from back east, and he'd done lots of reading. And, of course, I guess that's where we got our reading. Mother read quite a bit, especially cookbooks or recipes, and I read it. I never had money for it.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: So he started getting the newspaper every day?

speaker: Hattie Wilken Johnson
words: Yes, every day we had to go up to Cameron. Father would sit there on the porch, on the front porch, and he'd see where the mail carrier would come up to Cameron. Kids would have to hike up and get the papers.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: And then he'd sit and read them? He would sit on the porch and read them?

speaker: Hattie Wilken Johnson
words: Yes, he'd read them. And since we got the paper down from Cameron, that would be about four o 'clock or half past four. So he'd be sitting on the porch and read those papers.

speaker: Hattie Wilken Johnson
words: You know, one thing about the boys going to war that I think is interesting is that I have known some of the Swedish people and Norwegian people. They've told me that they didn't want their, that when they were kids, they didn't want to go into the war, into the first war. They didn't want to fight. They weren't for that. They didn't like going. Some of them. They thought they'd rather be on the farm, and they didn't think we ought to be in the war anyway. That was their attitude. But I was thinking that for folks that were German, maybe they would feel that they had to to fight to prove that they were loyal.

speaker: Hattie Wilken Johnson
words: Yes, they had to work. And the ones that were here, they were Americans. And of course they got meant for their families over there, their brothers and their relatives back there.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Do you think that the boys really wanted to go to fight? How? Do you think that the boys really wanted to go to fight?

speaker: Hattie Wilken Johnson
words: Well, it's just like they would go any place else, any other place.

speaker: Hattie Wilken Johnson
words: You know, it was just, they were called on and they had to obey the laws here. And of course, they didn't know whether they'd be in France or where. So much of the time, my brother, when he went over, that was in France. And there, when they come over from England to France, you know, when they made the invasion there, he was over in France then. And that was, they had to obey the flag. That was, and of course, Father, he was, he had to do what they do here in America if they belonged to this. He was an American and he joined the American people, you know, and that's, and of course, he felt bad about those over in Germany, those relatives. And they lost over there his brother's children and grandchildren, though they lost quite a few over there, two of those that had to go to war. We had pictures of them and I may have them with some of mine. I've never packed my pictures yet.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Do you think that they took more German boys than others?

speaker: Hattie Wilken Johnson
words: I know from here. Yeah. They took them one alike and one another. They were, if they, who had whatever community they was in, they took them. They were up to 18, anyone's 18, why they had to go. And it didn't make any difference whether they were German or English or any other. That was the most of the people that were around. There were others that were French descent. Some of them had some of them that were of other descent, you know.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Did all the boys go who were called?

speaker: Hattie Wilken Johnson
words: All of them that when they were called, they went. Yes, there was no other way unless they were sent down there to the doctors at Lush. They don't examine if they were able to go, you know, or physically able to go. They had to go. And some of them, of course, they wouldn't be able to go. But they would be something, be wrong, they'd be lame, or their eyes or something, you know, that they couldn't go.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: I wanted to ask you about, do you remember the Ku Klux Klan in the 1920s?

speaker: Hattie Wilken Johnson
words: Yes.

speaker: Hattie Wilken Johnson
words: There weren't so many of those in this country here. But back in the North and South Carolina's and Georgia and down back in there, there were a lot of those Ku Klux Klan. And some of them, they came, there was a doctorate at Peck. He'd come from that part of the country, and his brother was a pharmacist. And he'd had lots about doctors too, they'd get there. But he was with them back in North Carolina. And he had to leave there. He went to Denver someplace that his family came with. Oh, quite a few years after that, they came to Peck.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Was he in the Klan at Peck?

speaker: Hattie Wilken Johnson
words: Oh, no, there was no Klan at Peck. They didn't have any in there. I don't know of any of it around here. Oh, I think there was some around here. I don't think it was a lot. There were some, but some of them around, like I was telling you about Lelandville, Citrus Silver. I tried to belittle the Germans up there, the church, like taking that flag down.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Were some of them in the Klan?

speaker: Hattie Wilken Johnson
words: I don't think there was any in the Klan. They were kind of together, we called them. Crazy nuts or something, I don't know what to...

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: You mean there were a group?

speaker: Hattie Wilken Johnson
words: There was a group of them. But I don't think there was just a solid big group or not, but there was just some of them.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: But it wasn't the Klan?

speaker: Hattie Wilken Johnson
words: No, there was no Klan. There was never any Klan or anything like that that I know of. I don't think there's any not around here, this part of the country. I never heard of it. I had heard there was a small group in... Well, there were small groups of them, like that minister that I was telling you about, that tried to look down on them or try to do what they could to know. But the trouble was that those Germans, you know, a lot of them, they bought those war bonds when the time came, and they bought more than some of the other ones because they had the money and they put it in there. And their father bought one for me. Of course, I had the money saved up, I could buy myself one.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Were the people doing pretty well at that time? Were they farming?

speaker: Hattie Wilken Johnson
words: Yes, they were farming and they had cattle and they could sell the grain and everything.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: So they had money?

speaker: Hattie Wilken Johnson
words: They had the money and they had the bees that they sold, and we sold honey. And father worked on the grains on the roads. And there we worked all the time.

speaker: Hattie Wilken Johnson
words: I think I should get going. I feel like I've stayed...

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: What time is it?

speaker: Hattie Wilken Johnson
words: Well, I don't know.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: What does your watch say?

speaker: Hattie Wilken Johnson
words: Well, he was the head man around here. They had Vollmer this and Vollmer that. And Vollmer, Troy was Vollmer for a long time. And then Vollmer over at Craigmont now. It used to be Vollmer, then another town. They were just about that far apart as the road went between them. They did find the United Craigmont.

speaker: Hattie Wilken Johnson
words: I know some places people complain that Vollmer foreclosed on people and took... he got to be a big landowner that way.

speaker: Hattie Wilken Johnson
words: I suppose he was. I don't know so much about it. I never heard him tell so much about it. I know father, he was with the other farmers. They built their own warehouses here. I know that Vollmer was one of the first ones here. And when they built Kendrick, he was one of the head ones down here to get the railroad down and all that.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: But you didn't hear people talk about him, what kind of a person he was?

speaker: Hattie Wilken Johnson
words: Not so much because we lived up there. Of course I was just growing up.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: He was awfully wealthy, wasn't he? I think he became a millionaire.

speaker: Hattie Wilken Johnson
words: Oh, I think that he was. I didn't get with him in some of those banks.

speaker: Hattie Wilken Johnson
words: Like the Genesee Bank was his bank.

speaker: Hattie Wilken Johnson
words: Yes, and I guess he had the banks different places. Yes, he had lots of money, Vollmer. Of course I was just growing up then. It didn't mean much to me.
